Each migration is stored as a separate file with upgrade() and downgrade() functions and a reference to its parent revision, forming a directed chain. revision creates such a file, downgrade applies the reverse changes, and heads lists revisions with no descendants. If there are several branches and more than one head appears, they're reconciled with the merge command.

cards.front: Adding a column to a loaded table without downtime
cards.back: The command itself is trivial - ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN delivery_date DATE. The danger lies in the lock and in rewriting the table. In older engines, ADD COLUMN ... DEFAULT could rewrite every row under an exclusive lock and stall writes to the table. So the safe scenario is: add a nullable column without a DEFAULT (a fast metadata operation), then, if needed, backfill the values in batches, and only after that, as a separate step, add NOT NULL/the index. In PostgreSQL 11+ and MySQL 8 a constant DEFAULT is already added instantly, but checking the behavior of the specific version is a must.

cards.front: What a transaction is and why group operations together
cards.back: A transaction combines several changes into a unit of work with ACID guarantees. The classic example is a money transfer: a debit from one account and a credit to another must happen together. If something fails after the debit, ROLLBACK undoes everything, and consistency isn't broken. COMMIT confirms, ROLLBACK cancels, and the start of the transaction sets the boundaries.

cards.front: When NoSQL beats relational databases, and when it loses
cards.back: NoSQL is not "a database better than SQL" but a different set of trade-offs. I choose NoSQL when the data is loosely structured or its shape changes often, when I need linear scalability across many nodes, and when the workload is simple key-based reads and writes over terabytes of data. I stay on a relational DBMS when ACID transactions, complex joins and analytics are critical, or when the data model is stable and maps well onto tables. So it's not fashion that decides, but the workload profile and consistency requirements.

cards.front: How to check how many clients are currently connected to a DBMS
cards.back: Every DBMS exposes its own connection metrics, and I look them up through service views rather than guessing. In PostgreSQL I run SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity; — this view holds one row per active backend. In MySQL I check SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Threads_connected';. This matters so you don't hit max_connections: once the limit is exhausted, new clients are refused, so monitoring connections is an early warning about the pool, a connection leak, or a sudden traffic spike.

cards.front: Counting rows in a table: exact COUNT and a fast estimate
cards.back: If you need accuracy, that's SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table;. I understand that on millions of rows such a query has to walk every row (or the index), and in PostgreSQL it also has to check each row's visibility because of MVCC, so it isn't cheap. When precision isn't critical — say, for a dashboard or a rough size check — I take an estimate from system statistics: reltuples from pg_class in PostgreSQL, or a row from information_schema.tables in MySQL. It's nearly instant, at the cost of being approximate and dependent on how fresh the statistics are.

cards.front: The four transaction isolation levels and the anomalies they cut off
cards.back: Isolation is about which anomalies of concurrent transactions are allowed. There are three anomalies: dirty read (seeing uncommitted changes), non-repeatable read (the same row changes between two SELECTs), and phantoms (new rows appear in a range you've already read). The four levels close them off one by one: Read Uncommitted protects against nothing, Read Committed removes dirty reads, Repeatable Read removes non-repeatable reads too, and Serializable also excludes phantoms, giving behavior as if transactions ran one after another. The stricter the level, the more locks and rollbacks, so the default is usually Read Committed, and you raise the bar only where it's truly needed.

cards.front: Database sharding: how horizontal data splitting removes bottlenecks
cards.back: Sharding is horizontal scaling of storage: instead of beefing up a single server, we split the data by a sharding key across several independent databases. Each shard serves its own range or hash of keys, so queries and writes are distributed across nodes. This gives us higher throughput and resilience: one shard going down doesn't take the whole system with it. We pay for it with added complexity: queries that touch several shards, and maintaining consistency.

cards.front: Indexes: what you pay for faster reads and why writes get more expensive
cards.back: An index is an ordered auxiliary structure over one or more columns, working like a book's index: instead of walking every row, the database descends the tree in logarithmic time. The benefit shows up in filtering, sorting, and joins on indexed columns. The flip side: each index takes up space and must be kept up to date on every write, so INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE get slower. The takeaway: index what you actually search by often, not everything in sight.

cards.front: Normalization vs. denormalization: where to remove duplicates and where to deliberately bring them back
cards.back: Normalization is a set of schema-design rules that removes data duplication and protects against insert, update, and delete anomalies. It's achieved by splitting into tables and linking them by keys: 1NF requires atomic values, 2NF requires non-key columns to depend on the whole key, 3NF requires no transitive dependencies. Denormalization goes the other way: we deliberately duplicate data to cut down on JOINs and speed up reads. It's justified in analytics and data marts, where reads happen far more often than writes. By default you design normalized, and you denormalize selectively against a proven performance problem.

cards.front: A slow SQL query: how to catch the culprit and read the execution plan
cards.back: First you need to find the problem query: through monitoring (pg_stat_statements in PostgreSQL, Performance Schema in MySQL) or the slow query log. Next is EXPLAIN (or EXPLAIN ANALYZE), which shows the plan: which indexes are used, how many rows are planned to be processed, and where a full table scan happens. Typical causes: no index on the columns in WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY, the query pulls extra rows or columns, or stale statistics throw off the optimizer. Fixes: add a suitable index, rewrite the query (JOIN instead of a subquery, LIMIT), refresh statistics via ANALYZE.

cards.front: Combining data from two tables without the JOIN operator: what are the options?
cards.back: Here's how I'd put it: JOIN is not the only tool. There's the subquery: an inner SELECT returns a set of keys from one table, and the outer query selects rows from the other by them via IN or EXISTS. The second route is to pull both result sets separately and stitch them together on the application side, for example through a dictionary keyed by the join key. The subquery keeps the logic in the database; assembling in code is handy when you need non-standard processing that's hard to express in SQL. The downside of manual assembly is two round trips to the database and the risk of leaving the join up to the application.

cards.front: Why call scalars() in SQLAlchemy and what does it change in the result?
cards.back: By default session.execute(stmt) returns a Result object where each row is a tuple (Row), even when a single column is selected. To get ['Alice', 'Bob'] instead of [('Alice',), ('Bob',)], I call result.scalars(). It takes the first element of each row and returns a ScalarResult, on which I can then chain .all(), .first(), .one(), or iteration. This matters especially with the ORM: with select(User) and no scalars() I get tuples with objects inside, whereas with scalars() I get a list of User instances directly. There's also a shortcut: session.scalars(stmt).

cards.front: Finding and analyzing a slow query in PostgreSQL: where to start?
cards.back: I work in two stages. First I look for a candidate: I enable pg_stat_statements and see which queries produce the highest total and average execution time - this gives a picture across the whole workload rather than a single call. Once the culprit is found, I move on to analyzing the plan: EXPLAIN shows the plan without running it, while EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes the query and reports the real time and row counts for each node. In the plan, the first things I look at are a Seq Scan where an index was expected, a mismatch between estimated and actual rows, and expensive joins. From there it's an index, a rewritten query, or tweaked settings.

cards.front: Database locks: what they are and what role they play
cards.back: I explain it through the problem: when transactions run in parallel, anomalies arise without coordination - dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, lost updates, phantoms. Locks coordinate access. There's a shared (S) lock for reads, which several transactions can hold at once, but nobody can write. And there's an exclusive (X) lock for writes, held by only one transaction while the rest wait. Locks can apply at the row, page, table, or whole-database level: the finer the granularity, the higher the concurrency, but the greater the overhead. The transaction isolation level is precisely what determines which locks are taken and when. The flip side is deadlocks, where two transactions wait on each other's locks.

cards.front: How do you make a single column effectively immutable in PostgreSQL?
cards.back: I'd say Postgres has no built-in immutability flag for a column. I implement write protection with a trigger: a BEFORE UPDATE function compares NEW.salary with OLD.salary via IS DISTINCT FROM and raises an exception if the value is being changed. If the field additionally needs to be hidden from reads, I hand the user a view without that column rather than the table itself, and lock down direct access to the base table with privileges. The trigger covers writes, the view covers reads - together they give "read-only" at the level of a single field.

cards.front: How do you build a SELECT so it never touches restricted fields?
cards.back: The first rule is not to write SELECT *, but to list specific columns: that way the query doesn't pull in fields the user has no access to, and it transfers less data too. But relying on developer discipline alone is risky, so I lock access down on the database side. I create a view containing only the public fields and grant the user privileges on the view rather than the underlying table. For complex access rules I move the logic into a function that returns a table with the allowed set of columns. Then any SELECT * over that view or function returns only what's permitted.

cards.front: What problems come up with replication in PostgreSQL?
cards.back: The main problem is replica lag: by default replication is asynchronous, and for some time the replica serves stale data. For "read right after write" scenarios this breaks the logic, and you have to either read from the master or enable synchronous replication - which slows commits down, because the master waits for confirmation. Next: you can't write to a replica, or it'll desync, so it must be strictly read-only. On top of that, replication requires monitoring of lag and stream state, plus extra CPU, memory, and disk per replica. Roughly: you're choosing between data freshness, write speed, and cost.

cards.front: Connection pool in SQLAlchemy: what it solves and what it's built from
cards.back: Establishing a new TCP and authentication connection to the database costs milliseconds and resources on both sides. The pool solves this like so: when the engine is created, SQLAlchemy sets up a set of reusable connections (QueuePool by default). When code needs a connection, it takes a free one from the pool; when the work is done and the connection is closed at the application level, the physical connection isn't torn down but returned to the pool. With the pool_size, max_overflow, and pool_timeout parameters I tune the base size, the allowed overflow, and the wait time for a free connection.

cards.front: Where to keep a query cache: Redis or a table in PostgreSQL
cards.back: This isn't a question of "which is better," but a question of requirements. If the cache is hot, ephemeral, and must be served instantly - my choice is Redis: in-memory, key-value, built-in TTL, and memory-based eviction. But if the "cache" must be durable, read in a single transaction with the rest of the data, or take part in analytical queries - I keep it in PostgreSQL, because Redis doesn't provide the ACID guarantees of a relational DB. In practice a common pattern is both layers: PostgreSQL as the source of truth, Redis as a fast cache in front of it.

cards.front: Primary key vs foreign key: what is the fundamental difference
cards.back: A primary key answers the question "how do I uniquely identify this row": it is unique, disallows NULL, and the DBMS builds an index for it. A foreign key answers the question "which row in another table am I pointing to": it stores a primary-key value from the parent table, and the database makes sure that such a value actually exists there. Roughly: a PK is about the identity of a record, an FK is about the relationship between records. A table has exactly one PK, but it can have several FKs.

cards.front: How to model a one-to-many relationship in a relational database
cards.back: I put the foreign key in the table on the "many" side, not the "one". The classic example is an author and books: one author writes many books, each book has one author. So the Books table gets an AuthorID column - a foreign key to Authors.AuthorID. No separate junction table is needed here (that is only required for "many-to-many"). Through the FK the database makes sure every book references an existing author, and a JOIN on this field brings the data back together.

cards.front: How to implement a one-to-one relationship between tables?
cards.back: "A one-to-one relationship means that each row in table A corresponds to at most one row in table B. In plain SQL I put a foreign key to the parent in the subordinate table and add UNIQUE to it - it is precisely the uniqueness that turns an ordinary foreign key into a 1:1. In Django there is OneToOneField for this; under the hood it does exactly the same thing: an FK plus a unique index. I use this approach when I want to move rarely used or bulky fields into a separate table without breaking the logical link to the main record."

cards.front: Where relational databases excel, and where they start to lose?
cards.back: "A relational database is about guarantees. I choose it when I need ACID transactions, referential integrity through keys, and rich SQL for analytics: the data will not drift apart, and complex queries with JOINs are written declaratively. The price of these guarantees is a rigid schema and difficult horizontal scaling. When I have a stream of loosely structured documents, gigantic volumes, or need linear scalability across many nodes, I look toward NoSQL. So the choice is not 'better/worse' but a matter of the nature of the data and the consistency requirements."

cards.front: Where does the N+1 query problem come from and how to fix it?
cards.back: "The N+1 problem arises from lazy loading of relationships in the ORM. I fetch the list of authors with one query, and then in a loop I access author.books - and for each author the ORM quietly makes a separate SQL query. Ten authors turn into eleven queries, a hundred into a hundred and one. I solve this with eager loading: with a single JOIN or a dedicated ORM method (joinedload/selectinload in SQLAlchemy, select_related/prefetch_related in Django) I pull the related rows in advance. The main thing is to be able to spot N+1 in the query log rather than by eye."

cards.front: DELETE vs TRUNCATE: what is the practical difference?
cards.back: "I use DELETE when I need to delete selectively - with WHERE - or when delete triggers must run. It is row-by-row and logged, so it can be rolled back within a transaction, but it is slow on large tables. I use TRUNCATE when I need to quickly empty a whole table: it does not delete rows one by one but frees the data pages, also resetting the auto-increment counter. It does not support conditions and does not call per-row triggers. Roughly: targeted and conditional cleanup is DELETE, a full table reset is TRUNCATE."

cards.front: Alembic: why you need it for versioning the database schema
cards.back: Alembic solves the problem of managed schema evolution. Any structural change - a new table, a renamed column, a dropped index - is captured as a separate revision with upgrade and downgrade functions. Revisions are kept in a linear (or branching) history and applied in order. Because of this, dev, staging, and production reach an identical structure using the same set of commands, with no manual SQL transfer and no drift. With --autogenerate Alembic also compares the SQLAlchemy models against the current schema and drafts a migration for you.

cards.front: How to find and speed up slow SQL queries: profiling in practice
cards.back: Profiling is needed to move from the guess "the query is slow" to facts. I look at the execution plan via EXPLAIN, and the real timings and number of processed rows via EXPLAIN ANALYZE. The plan shows the main things: whether the database uses an index or does a full table scan (seq scan), how the tables are joined, whether the planner underestimated the data volume. Then I act on the facts: add a missing index, rewrite the query so the condition becomes sargable, or change the join order. After each change I rerun EXPLAIN ANALYZE and compare the cost and the actual time.

cards.front: Database cursor: row-by-row access to a query result
cards.back: A cursor is useful wherever a query result cannot or should not be loaded in full. A query might return millions of rows - the cursor hands them over in portions as you iterate, so memory is spent only on the current block. The lifecycle is simple: create the cursor, execute the query, read rows (fetchone/fetchmany/iteration), close it. In Python drivers (DB-API), the cursor is also the entry point for running SQL: cursor.execute(...) followed by reading. It's important to distinguish client-side and server-side cursors: a server-side cursor (for example, a named cursor in psycopg2) really does not pull the whole result to the client, whereas an ordinary buffered cursor may.

cards.front: Nested transactions and SAVEPOINT: partial rollback inside a single transaction
cards.back: Most relational DBMSs have no true "transactions within a transaction" - they have SAVEPOINT. You set a save point inside an open transaction, and if something goes wrong afterward, you run ROLLBACK TO savepoint: only the changes made after that point are rolled back, while the work already done in the outer transaction is preserved. This gives modularity and flexible error handling: you break a complex operation into parts, and a failure in one part does not bring down the whole transaction. The final COMMIT commits everything at once. Important: until the outer COMMIT is done, nothing is committed - a savepoint lives only inside the current transaction.

cards.front: Which set of SQL commands provides transaction control?
cards.back: The basic set for transaction control is BEGIN (or START TRANSACTION), COMMIT, and ROLLBACK. The first command starts a transaction, the second locks in the result and makes it visible to other sessions, and the third undoes all accumulated changes. For fine-grained control you add SAVEPOINT with ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT - they let you roll back only part of the work inside a transaction without discarding it entirely. And SET TRANSACTION sets the isolation level and the access mode. Together these provide atomicity: either all operations go through, or none do.

cards.front: How do the JOIN types differ and what result does each produce?
cards.back: A JOIN is needed to assemble into one result data that is spread across different tables. INNER JOIN returns only rows that have a match in both tables - it's the workhorse. LEFT JOIN keeps all rows of the left table and substitutes NULL wherever there is no match on the right; RIGHT JOIN does the same mirrored for the right table. FULL OUTER JOIN combines the results of LEFT and RIGHT: all rows of both tables remain, and unmatched slots are filled with NULL. CROSS JOIN uses no join condition at all and pairs every row with every other - it's used rarely because of the explosive growth in the number of rows.

cards.front: What are the main challenges of PostgreSQL replication and what should you keep in mind?
cards.back: PostgreSQL replication copies data from the master to replicas for fault tolerance, read scaling, and availability. The main trade-off is between asynchronous mode (the master doesn't wait for acknowledgment, replicas may lag, but writes are fast) and synchronous mode (the master waits for acknowledgment, data is consistent, but writes are slower). In an interview it's worth naming the key problems: replica lag, data consistency, the absence of built-in automatic failover (it's set up via Patroni or pgpool), overhead on the master, and the need for constant monitoring via pg_stat_replication. Separately - upgrades and migrations: without planning, you can easily end up with downtime or desynchronization.

cards.front: Why does an engineer need Big O to evaluate an algorithm?
cards.back: Big O is there to predict in advance how an algorithm will behave on large data and to compare two solutions without running them. I don't look at absolute speed, but at how many times the cost will grow if the data becomes, say, ten times larger. The key classes I keep in mind are O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), and O(n^2) — that's enough to assess most interview problems.

cards.front: Which is faster to look up by key — a list or a dictionary?
cards.back: For lookup by key a dictionary is fundamentally faster: the key is run through a hash function, and Python jumps straight to the slot where the value sits — that's amortized O(1). A list doesn't offer such luxury: to check whether a value is present, it goes from the start and compares elements one by one, that is O(n). So I use lists where order matters or where duplicates are needed, and dictionaries when I need to retrieve data by a unique key frequently.

cards.front: What do the basic operations on list, dict, set, and deque cost?
cards.back: I keep a map of complexities in my head: a list is good for index access and appending to the end (O(1)), but bad for insertions in the middle (O(n)). A set and a dictionary are built on hash tables, so their addition, removal, and membership check are on average O(1). If I need a queue or operations at both ends, I use deque instead of a list, because list.pop(0) is O(n) while deque.popleft() is O(1). I choose the structure based on the dominant operation of the task.

cards.front: Binary tree: how is it structured and what is it for?
cards.back: A binary tree is nodes, each of which has at most two children. A node without a parent is the root; nodes without children are leaves. The structure itself is a foundation: binary search trees (BST) are built on it, where values to the left of a node are smaller and to the right are larger, as well as heaps for priority queues. In a balanced BST, search, insertion, and removal run in O(log n), because at each step we cut off half the tree.

cards.front: How fast does a hash table find a value by key?
cards.back: "Lookup by key is on average O(1): the hash function translates the key into an array index, and we land in the right cell in constant time. But that's the average under a uniform distribution and a reasonable load factor. If the hash function is poor or all keys yield the same hash, the bucket turns into a linear structure, and lookup degrades to O(n). That's why in practice both the quality of the hash function and timely table resizing matter."

cards.front: What does bubble sort cost in time and memory?
cards.back: "Bubble sort is a stable in-place sort. Time: average and worst case O(n^2), since two nested passes give on the order of n*n comparisons. The best case O(n) is achievable if you add a flag and break out of the loop when a pass makes no swaps - then a sorted input is processed in a single pass. Memory is O(1): apart from a couple of temporary variables, nothing is needed. In practice it isn't used because of the quadratic asymptotics, but it's handy as a teaching example."

cards.front: In what ways does a hash table resolve key collisions?
cards.back: "A collision is when two different keys map to the same index. It is resolved in two ways. The first is chaining: each slot leads to a linked list (or tree), and colliding keys are appended there; simple and resilient to high occupancy. The second is open addressing: everything sits right in the array, and on a collision the next free slot is found by a deterministic scheme. Probing schemes: linear (step +1), quadratic (the step grows quadratically), and double hashing (the step is set by a second hash function). Chaining is simpler and tolerates a high load factor; open addressing is friendlier to the cache but suffers from clustering."

cards.front: When is a linked list better than an array?
cards.back: "A list beats an array where insertions and deletions in the middle are frequent: with a reference to a node, we relink a couple of pointers in O(1), without shifting the rest of the elements. It's also handy when the volume of data isn't known up front - the size changes on the fly. The price is O(n) index access (you have to walk from the head) and the overhead of storing pointers plus poor cache locality. An array, on the contrary, gives O(1) index access and is compact in memory, but insertion/deletion in the middle is O(n). The choice is a trade-off between fast access and cheap structural modification."

cards.front: Stack vs heap: where do a program's variables and objects live?
cards.back: The stack is memory for call frames: on each function call a frame with local variables and a return address is pushed, and on exit the frame is popped. It works strictly LIFO, allocation and freeing are instant, but the size is limited - hence a stack overflow on overly deep recursion. The heap is the region for dynamic data and long-lived objects; whatever must exist longer than a single call goes there. Access to the heap is slower because it works through pointers, and freeing errors lead to leaks. In Python the name-references live in frames, while the objects themselves live in the heap, and their lifetime is determined by reference counting and the garbage collector.

cards.front: Array or linked list: what do we pay for fast access and for fast insertion?
cards.back: It's a compromise between access speed and the cost of changes. An array stores data in a contiguous block, the address of any element is computed from the index, so reading is O(1). But insertion or deletion in the middle requires shifting the tail, that's O(n). A linked list is a chain of nodes, each knowing the next (and in a doubly linked one, the previous too); adding or removing a node when you have a reference to a neighbor is O(1), but index access is O(n), because you have to walk from the head. Another nuance: a list spends extra memory on pointers, while an array packs more compactly and is friendlier to the processor cache. By the way, list in Python is a dynamic array, not a linked list.

cards.front: What is the time cost of iterating over every element of a list?
cards.back: Iterating over a list is O(n), where n is the number of elements, because the loop walks through every element once. Double the list's length and the time roughly doubles too. The key thing here is not to confuse the complexity of the traversal itself with the complexity of what happens inside the loop: if each iteration runs an O(k) operation, the overall complexity becomes O(n*k). For example, if x in other_list inside the loop turns a harmless traversal into O(n*m). But the traversal on its own, without nested heavy work, is honest O(n).

cards.front: What is under the hood of a Python dict, and why is key lookup so fast?
cards.back: A dict is a hash table. When you put in a pair, Python computes hash(key), uses it to determine the index of a slot in the internal array, and stores the entry there. Lookup works the same way: the same hash leads straight to the right slot, with no scanning, hence the average O(1). If two different keys map to the same index, that is a collision, and CPython resolves it with open addressing (probing), finding another free slot. This is why keys must be hashable and stable under hashing - a list cannot be a key, but a tuple of immutable elements can. In the worst case, with massive collisions, operations degrade to O(n), but in practice this is rare. And separately: starting from Python 3.7, a dictionary is guaranteed to preserve insertion order.

cards.front: Recursion versus a loop: what is the fundamental difference?
cards.back: Recursion and iteration are interchangeable in their result but differ in implementation and resource use. Recursion describes a solution in terms of itself and must have a base case, otherwise it runs forever and hits the stack limit. Each nested call takes a frame on the stack, so deep recursion in Python fails with a RecursionError at around a thousand levels. Iteration keeps state in ordinary variables and does not grow the stack, so in terms of memory and constants it is almost always more efficient. I choose recursion where the structure of the problem is tree-like (tree traversal, divide-and-conquer splitting), and iteration for linear passes and when performance matters.

cards.front: Merge sort: how does sorting via splitting and merging work?
cards.back: Merge sort splits the array in two, recursively sorts each half, and merges them in linear time. The recursion depth is log n levels, and at each level the merge scans all n elements, which gives O(n log n) both on average and in the worst case - unlike quicksort, whose worst case is O(n²). The algorithm is stable: equal elements keep their original order. We pay for this with O(n) memory for a temporary merge buffer. Because of its predictable complexity and stability, merge sort is chosen for external sorting of large data and as the basis for hybrids like Timsort.

cards.front: Indexing in Python: how do you get an element by its position?
cards.back: In Python you access a sequence element by index using []. Forward numbering starts at 0, so the first element is seq[0], and for a list of length n the last is seq[n-1]. Python's convenience is in negative indices: seq[-1] is always the last, seq[-2] the second to last, and there is no need to compute the length by hand. Indexing works the same for lists, tuples, and strings, because they are all ordered sequences. For lists and tuples, access by index is O(1): under the hood is an array of pointers, and the position is computed by arithmetic. Going out of bounds raises an IndexError, and that is important to remember when working with user data.

cards.front: Cost of inserting into a Python list: O(1) or O(n)?
cards.back: A Python list is a dynamic array of pointers in a contiguous block of memory. That's why indexed access is O(1), but insertion depends on the location. append() usually just writes the element into the free tail in O(1); occasionally there isn't enough room, and Python allocates a larger block and copies everything into it in O(n), but these expensive resizes happen so rarely that on average (amortized) append stays O(1). Meanwhile insert(i, x) at an arbitrary position is forced to shift right all elements after index i in order to free up a cell — that's O(n). Inserting at the front, insert(0, x), is the worst case: every element shifts. If I need frequent insertions at the front, I reach for collections.deque, which is O(1) on both ends.

cards.front: Why does binary search run in O(log n)?
cards.back: Binary search applies only to sorted data. It takes the middle element, compares it with the target, and in a single step discards half of the range. Since the number of candidates is halved on each iteration, the total number of steps equals how many times n can be divided by 2 down to one, which is log₂n. Memory is O(1) for the iterative version. Compared with linear search at O(n) the gain is enormous: in an array of a million elements about 20 comparisons are enough.

cards.front: How much does it cost to fetch a value from a dictionary by key?
cards.back: A Python dictionary is built on a hash table. On a d[key] lookup the interpreter computes hash(key), uses it to calculate a position in the table, and fetches the value without scanning the other pairs — hence amortized O(1). Collisions (different keys, the same hash) are resolved with open addressing, and as they pile up access degrades, in the worst case to O(n). In practice this almost never happens: the table grows and is reallocated, keeping a low load factor. The same average O(1) holds for insertion and deletion.

cards.front: Which basic sorting algorithms should you know, and how do they differ?
cards.back: The simple quadratic sorts (bubble, insertion, selection) take a couple of lines to write, but they're unusable on large data because of O(n²). The efficient O(n log n) ones: quicksort (fast in practice, in-place, but its worst case is O(n²) with a bad pivot), merge sort (stable, guaranteed O(n log n), but requires O(n) extra memory), and heapsort (in-place, guaranteed O(n log n), unstable). In real code you call sorted() / list.sort() — that's Timsort, a hybrid of merge and insertion sort, stable and O(n log n).

cards.front: Why is indexed access O(1) in an array but O(n) in a linked list?
cards.back: The speed of indexed access is determined by the data layout in memory. An array is a contiguous block, and the address of an element is computed as base + index × element_size, in constant time. A Python list is internally a dynamic array of pointers, so lst[i] is also O(1). A linked list stores its nodes anywhere, each node knows only the next one, and the only way to reach index i is to make i hops along the links, which gives O(n). On the other hand, a linked list wins on insertion/deletion in the middle when you have a reference to the node: O(1) versus O(n) for an array.

cards.front: Why a dynamic array grows on its own and how it differs from a static one
cards.back: A dynamic array stores elements contiguously in memory, like a static one, but unlike a static array it can resize on the fly. When the allocated capacity is full, it allocates a new block (usually twice as large), copies the old elements there, and keeps going. Thanks to the capacity-doubling strategy, appending at the end stays amortized O(1): the rare expensive copies are spread out across many cheap additions. In Python, list is exactly such a structure.

cards.front: How a hash table finds a value almost instantly
cards.back: A hash table (hash map) stores key-value pairs. On write, it runs the key through a hash function, takes the remainder of division by the size of the internal array, and puts the value into the resulting bucket. On read, the same computation is performed - and the table goes straight to the right slot without scanning the rest. That is why the average complexity of operations is O(1). A problem arises when two different keys yield the same index (a collision); it is solved with chaining or open addressing. In the worst case, when everything lands in one bucket, operations degrade to O(n). key "Alice" hash() index = 2 [0] [1] [2] 555-1234

cards.front: Build your own list: how a dynamic array works under the hood
cards.back: list in Python is a dynamic array: elements lie contiguously in memory, and the block resizes as it grows. To reproduce it by hand, you need three things: a fixed-size buffer, a counter of occupied slots (size), and a declared capacity (capacity). On append I check whether there is room; if not, I double the capacity, copy the old elements into a new buffer, and only then insert. Deletion by index shifts the tail to the left. Access by index is O(1), appending at the end is amortized O(1), and deletion from the middle is O(n).

cards.front: Linear versus quadratic complexity: where the line is drawn
cards.back: Big-O describes how runtime scales as the input size n grows, ignoring constants. O(n) means that doubling the input roughly doubles the time. O(n²) means that doubling the input quadruples the time. So on a thousand elements a linear algorithm does ~1000 steps while a quadratic one does ~1,000,000: a thousandfold difference, and it only grows from there. In an interview it is important to stress: O speaks about asymptotics, not about specific seconds - on tiny n a quadratic algorithm with a small constant can even be faster, and what decides things is the behavior on large volumes.

cards.front: Greedy strategy: when the locally best choice fails at the global level
cards.back: A greedy algorithm builds the answer step by step: at each stage it takes the locally best available option and moves on without replaying previous steps. It works correctly only if the problem has the greedy-choice property (a local optimum leads to a global one) and optimal substructure. Classic correct examples are Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms, the fractional knapsack, and Huffman coding. But for problems like the 0/1 knapsack or making change with arbitrary denominations, greed breaks down, and there you need dynamic programming. The main thing I would stress in an interview: greed must be proven, not assumed.

cards.front: Inside a hash table: how a key turns into an address
cards.back: A hash table stores key-value pairs on top of an ordinary array. A hash function turns the key into a number, and the remainder of dividing it by the table size gives the bucket index. When two keys land in the same bucket, that is a collision, resolved either by chaining (the bucket holds a list of pairs) or by open addressing (you probe for the next free slot). The average complexity of the operations is O(1), but in the worst case, with a poor hash function, it degrades to O(n).

cards.front: Why is an `in` check faster for a dict than for a list?
cards.back: A list stores elements one after another, without any indexing by value. To find out whether an element is present, you have to compare it with each one in turn — hence O(n) in the worst case. A dict and a set are built as hash tables: a hash is computed from the key and points immediately to the bucket where the element should live. That is why an in check and key access are on average O(1). In the worst case (many collisions), degradation to O(n) is theoretically possible, but in practice it almost never happens.

cards.front: What is the difference between an array and a linked list
cards.back: An array sits in memory contiguously, so the address of any element is computed by arithmetic — access by index in O(1). We pay for this with shifting on insertion and deletion in the middle (O(n)) and a less flexible size. A linked list stores nodes anywhere, linking them by references: insertion and deletion mean rewriting a couple of references in O(1) if the position is already known, but access to the k-th element requires a walk from the head in O(n) and spends memory on the references themselves. The choice is a trade-off: an array for frequent access by index, a list for frequent insertions and deletions.

cards.front: Finding the N-th node from the end of a singly linked list in one pass
cards.back: A singly linked list has no access by index, so "the fifth from the end" done naively would require two passes: first find the length, then count back. I do it in one pass with two pointers. I space them out by the required gap: the leading fast goes ahead by 5 nodes, then I move fast and slow together one step at a time. The gap between them is preserved, so the moment fast reaches the end, slow lands on the fifth element from the end. The complexity is O(n) in time and O(1) in memory. I do not forget the case where there are fewer than five nodes.

cards.front: Merging dictionaries in Python and the fate of duplicate keys
cards.back: I pick the method by whether I need a new dictionary or an in-place edit. a | b and {**a, **b} create a new dictionary without touching the originals. a.update(b) changes a in place and returns None. In all variants the collision rule is the same: when a key matches, the value from the dictionary that comes further to the right wins. That is, in a | b the common key takes its value from b. This is a shallow merge: nested dictionaries are not merged recursively but replaced wholesale.

cards.front: Does the standard dict guarantee key iteration order?
cards.back: Starting with Python 3.7, the order of keys in a dict is guaranteed to match the order in which they were added - this is part of the language specification, not an accident of the implementation. Before 3.6 the order was undefined, and OrderedDict was used for predictable iteration. Today OrderedDict is only needed in special cases: when its specific methods matter (move_to_end, popitem(last=...)) or when you need order-aware comparison.

cards.front: How do you check whether a value occurs among a dictionary's values?
cards.back: value in my_dict checks for the presence of a key, not a value - you have to keep that in mind. To search by values, use value in my_dict.values(). An important note on complexity: looking up a key in a dictionary is O(1) on average, because under the hood it's a hash table, whereas iterating over values() is linear, O(n), since it's a sequential comparison of all values.

cards.front: Why is a tuple more compact in memory than a list of the same length?
cards.back: A list is a dynamic array: it is reallocated with spare room (over-allocation) so that append is O(1) on average, and it stores length and capacity separately. A tuple is immutable, its size is known at creation, so there is no extra capacity and the header structure is simpler. Hence the lower memory footprint and slightly faster access. The practical criterion for choosing: need mutability - use a list; need fixed data - use a tuple.

cards.front: How to remove duplicates from a list, and when order matters
cards.back: The shortest way is list(set(data)), but set does not preserve order, so the result may get shuffled. When order needs to be preserved, I use list(dict.fromkeys(data)): since Python 3.7 dictionaries keep insertion order, and keys are unique by definition. Both options work in O(n) and require the elements to be hashable.

cards.front: What can and cannot serve as dictionary keys
cards.back: Dictionary keys must be hashable. Access by key is built on a hash table: Python takes hash(key), uses it to select a bucket, and there compares keys with ==. So a key needs two properties — a stable hash for its entire lifetime and correct comparison. Immutable built-in types (str, int, float, bool, tuple, frozenset) satisfy this, while mutable ones (list, dict, set) do not, so trying to use them as a key raises TypeError: unhashable type.

cards.front: What does a hash table do when different keys produce the same hash?
cards.back: A collision is when the hash function returns the same index for two different keys. Collisions cannot be avoided entirely; they can only be handled gracefully. There are two classic approaches: chaining, where a single cell holds a list of all elements with that hash, and open addressing, where if a cell is occupied the element goes into the next free slot according to a probing rule. On average access stays O(1), but with a poor hash function it degrades to O(n).

cards.front: How to choose a collection for the task: where do you win on speed and memory?
cards.back: I start from the access pattern. If I need order and access by position - I take a list (O(1) access, but insertion in the middle is O(n)). If the data does not change and compactness matters - a tuple, it does not keep spare capacity for growth. If I often check "is this element present" or remove duplicates - a set, membership check is O(1). If I reach for a value by key - a dict, access is also O(1). For the speed of sets and dicts I pay with the memory for the hash table.

cards.front: What's under the hood of dict and set: why is access almost always O(1)?
cards.back: Both types are hash tables. A key is run through hash(), the result is reduced to an index in the bucket array, and the element is placed exactly there. That is why lookup does not depend on the collection size and on average costs O(1). When two keys fall into the same bucket - that is a collision; CPython resolves it with open addressing (probing). When the table fills up it grows and elements are relocated - this is amortized, so on average it is still O(1). The only difference between a set and a dict is that a set stores a key without a value.

cards.front: frozenset: when do you need an immutable set and what does it give you?
cards.back: frozenset is needed when you want a set that is at the same time hashable. A regular set is mutable and therefore not hashable - it cannot be a dict key or an element of another set. frozenset can do that: it has no add/remove, its hash is stable, so it works as a key or as an element of a nested set. All read operations (union, intersection, membership check) work just like with a regular set.

cards.front: A hashable object by hand: the __hash__ and __eq__ contract
cards.back: Hashability rests on the pair __hash__ and __eq__, bound by a contract: if two objects are equal by __eq__, their hashes must match. The reverse is not required - different objects may have the same hash, that is a normal collision. The hash must be constant for the entire lifetime of the object, so the fields that participate in it must not change. A handy trick is to compute the hash from a tuple of the same fields that are compared in __eq__. And the iron rule: if you override __eq__, override __hash__ too.

cards.front: append vs extend: a single element or merging an iterable element by element
cards.back: append increases the list length by one: whatever you pass becomes the last element. extend increases the length by the number of elements in the argument: it iterates over it and adds them one by one, effectively like +=. That's why append([1,2]) gives a nested list, while extend([1,2]) gives two new flat elements. Both methods modify the list in place and return None.

cards.front: How do you drop duplicates from a list, and when does the original order matter?
cards.back: If order doesn't matter - list(set(items)) is the shortest and fastest route, because membership checks in a set run in O(1). If order matters, since Python 3.7 I use list(dict.fromkeys(items)): a regular dict guarantees insertion order, and duplicates are cut off as identical keys. A manual loop with if x not in result also preserves order, but it's O(n^2) and only suitable for short lists.

cards.front: Why do you need slices, and what hides behind [start:stop:step]?
cards.back: A slice replaces manual iteration: instead of a loop, I grab the range I need in one line. The syntax is seq[start:stop:step], where start is included, stop is excluded, and step sets the stride and direction. For lists a slice returns a new list (a shallow copy), and for strings and tuples a new object of the same type. A negative step reverses the sequence: seq[::-1].

cards.front: Three ways to reverse a list: which is a copy, which an iterator, which a mutation?
cards.back: I choose based on whether I need a copy. lst[::-1] creates a new reversed list and leaves the original unchanged. reversed(lst) returns a lazy iterator - I wrap it in list() if I need a list; on the upside it works with any sequence and doesn't copy the data right away. lst.reverse() reverses in place, returns nothing (None), and saves memory. The main trap is that .reverse() mutates the original, while [::-1] and reversed() don't touch it.

cards.front: Three ways to drop an element from a list: where remove, where del, and where pop?
cards.back: In Python there are three different removal tools for three different situations. If I know the value and want to drop its first occurrence - I use list.remove(value). If I'm working with an index or a slice and don't need the returned value - I use the del operator, which can also delete the variable itself from the namespace. And if I need to both grab an element and remove it from the list at once - that's where only list.pop(index) fits; without an argument it pops the last element. The key thing to remember is the exceptions: remove raises ValueError when the value is absent, and pop raises IndexError when the index is out of bounds.

cards.front: You need a plain list of a dict's keys - how do you get it?
cards.back: The .keys() method returns a dict_keys view object, not a list. It is a dynamic "view" of the keys: it doesn't copy the data and reflects changes to the dictionary in real time. You can iterate over it directly in a loop, but if I need a real list - with indexing, slicing, or the ability to modify it independently of the dictionary - I explicitly convert it via list(my_dict.keys()). The same applies to .values() and .items(): all three return views, not lists.

cards.front: Two keys landed in the same slot: how does a hash table cope?
cards.back: A collision is the situation where the hash function produces the same index for two different keys. Collisions can't be avoided entirely, because there are infinitely many inputs and only a finite number of slots, so the table must be able to resolve them. There are two classic approaches. The first is chaining: each slot stores a collection (usually a linked list) of all elements with that index, and on a collision we simply append the new pair there. The second is open addressing: all elements live directly in the array, and when a slot is occupied we search for the next free one by a certain rule (linear probing, quadratic probing, or double hashing). CPython, by the way, uses exactly open addressing for its dict.

cards.front: Hashing, no fluff: what is it and why is it everywhere?
cards.back: A hash function takes data of arbitrary length as input and returns a string or number of fixed length - the hash. It has four key properties: determinism (the same input gives the same output), speed of computation, the avalanche effect (a tiny change to the input greatly changes the hash), and collision resistance (it's hard to find two inputs with the same hash). In practice, hashing solves three classes of problems: fast lookup via hash tables - this is what Python dictionaries and sets are built on; data integrity checking - you compare the hash of a downloaded file with the reference one and make sure it wasn't corrupted; and password storage - the database holds not the passwords themselves but their hashes, so that a leak doesn't reveal the originals.

cards.front: How many operations does a dict take to find and store a value by key?
cards.back: A dictionary is a hash table, so on average both insertion and reading by key work in constant time O(1) and don't depend on the number of elements. Degradation to O(n) is possible only under mass collisions, when many different keys land in the same chain; in practice a good hash function and recreating the table as it grows prevent that.

cards.front: What set of operations does the set type provide for working with unique elements?
cards.back: A set supports set-theoretic operations, each available both as an operator and as an identically named method: union | / union, intersection & / intersection, difference - / difference, symmetric difference ^ / symmetric_difference. Relationships are checked with comparison operators: = / issuperset. Since the elements are hashed, all these operations work fast, and the elements themselves must be hashable.

cards.front: Why can a hash function produce the same hash for different data?
cards.back: A collision is when the hashes of two distinct inputs coincide. It is inevitable, because the set of possible inputs is practically infinite while the range of hashes is finite (the Dirichlet principle, also known as the "pigeonhole principle"). That is why a hash table does not rely on the uniqueness of the hash: on a match it applies collision resolution - most often separate chaining or open addressing.

cards.front: What set of operations is available on mappings in Python?
cards.back: A dictionary supports the full set of mapping operations: writing by key (d[k] = v) both adds and updates at once, del d[k] and d.pop(k) delete, the in operator checks for the presence of a key in O(1), and the methods keys(), values(), items() return view objects for iteration. An important detail: all the basic operations - insertion, deletion, lookup - are O(1) on average thanks to the hash table under the hood.

cards.front: How do you merge two lists - keys and values - into a single dictionary?
cards.back: The idiomatic way is dict(zip(keys, values)). zip walks both lists in parallel and emits (key, value) tuples, and dict assembles a dictionary from those pairs. The solution reads in a second and runs in O(n). A nuance interviewers like to ask about: zip stops at the shortest list, so with differing lengths the tail of the longer one is lost without an error.

cards.front: How do you sort a list of dictionaries by the value of one of the fields?
cards.back: Via sorted() with the key argument: sorted(people, key=lambda x: x['age']). The key function is called once for each element and determines which value to compare by. To reverse, add reverse=True; to sort by several fields, return a tuple from key. An alternative to the lambda is operator.itemgetter('age'), which is slightly faster and reads cleaner. The sort is stable, so elements with an equal key keep their original order.

cards.front: Why a middleware layer exists and how it intercepts a request
cards.back: In Django, middleware are components that every request passes through on the way to a view and every response passes through on the way back. They are arranged in a stack (MIDDLEWARE in settings), and order matters: on the way in the request flows top to bottom, on the way out the response flows bottom to top. I use middleware for tasks common to all views: checking the session, adding security headers, measuring timing, unified exception handling. This keeps views focused on business logic, while cross-functional concerns are handled centrally.

cards.front: How Django splits an application into Model, View, and Template
cards.back: MVT is Django's flavor of MVC, where the framework itself (the URL router) plays the controller role, and the "View" layer in Django terms handles application logic. The Model describes the data structure and encapsulates database access through the ORM. The View receives the request, pulls data from the models, and chooses which template to render. The Template is HTML with the Django template language, which turns data into finished markup. The key idea is to separate data, logic, and presentation so each layer can change independently. Request View Model Template Response

cards.front: The path of an HTTP request inside Django: from URL to response
cards.back: The request lifecycle in Django is as follows: the request first passes through the inbound middleware stack, then the URL resolver picks a view from the URLconf by matching the path against urlpatterns. The view is a function or class where the application logic lives: if needed it reaches out to models, which run database queries through the ORM. The view builds an HttpResponse, usually by rendering a template with data. On the way back, the response passes through middleware again and is sent to the client. The main point is to understand that the URLconf routes, the view holds the logic, the model is responsible for data, and the template for markup.

cards.front: What contract WSGI defines and how it separates the web server from the Python application
cards.back: WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface) is a synchronous interface between a web server and a Python application, described in PEP 3333. The application here is any callable with two parameters: an environ dictionary (everything about the incoming request) and a start_response callback (used to set the status code and headers). In return, the application yields an iterable of body bytes. It's precisely this single protocol that lets Django or Flask run under Gunicorn, uWSGI, or Waitress with no code changes. WSGI doesn't cover async scenarios - for those there is ASGI.

cards.front: Customizing the user in Django: User, AbstractUser, or AbstractBaseUser
cards.back: Name the three options in increasing order of freedom. You take User from django.contrib.auth when the standard fields are enough. You inherit from AbstractUser to add your own attributes without losing the built-in model's behavior. You choose AbstractBaseUser when you need to redefine the whole structure - for example, to log in by email; then you'll have to write your own BaseUserManager and set up permission fields. And the key practical point: set AUTH_USER_MODEL from the start of the project, even if your custom model is empty for now, because swapping the user out on a project with data is an expensive and risky operation.

cards.front: A custom user model in Django: where to start and what to declare
cards.back: I set up my own user model before the project's first migration, because changing it later is extremely painful. I take AbstractBaseUser if I need email login and full control over the fields, or AbstractUser when augmenting the ready-made model is enough. I always attach my own BaseUserManager with create_user and create_superuser methods, specify USERNAME_FIELD and REQUIRED_FIELDS, and then register the model in AUTH_USER_MODEL. That way Django picks up my model everywhere: in the admin, in request.user, in forms.

cards.front: WebSockets in Django: why you need Channels and how to assemble the ASGI stack
cards.back: Plain Django is synchronous and works on a request-response model, so it can't hold a persistent WebSocket connection. For real-time I bring in Django Channels: it moves the application onto ASGI, runs under an async server like Daphne or Uvicorn, and shuttles messages between processes through a channel layer (usually Redis). I describe the connection logic in a consumer - a class with connect, disconnect, and receive methods. I declare WebSocket routes separately from ordinary URLs and gather everything in a ProtocolTypeRouter, which splits http and websocket into different branches.

cards.front: Business logic versus presentation: the model or the DRF serializer
cards.back: I keep business logic close to the data - in the model or in a separate service layer, not in the serializer. The reason is simple: a serializer is tied to a specific API endpoint, and if a discount calculation or a stock check lives in it, then when called from the admin, a management command, or a background task that rule simply won't apply. The serializer is responsible for the shape of the data on the wire: which fields to return, how to transform them, how to validate the incoming format. The model is responsible for the data being correct regardless of who touches it.

cards.front: Creating a record in the Django ORM: create() vs the constructor and save()
cards.back: If I need to create an object and save it immediately, I use Model.objects.create(**fields) - it's a single operation and a single INSERT query. When I need to do something with the object between creating and persisting it (compute a field, validate, set up relations), I create the instance with the constructor Model(**fields) and call save() later. One important nuance: create() always hits the database, whereas a bare Model(...) does not write the object to the database - until save(), it isn't there.

cards.front: What role does manage.py play in a Django project
cards.back: Put it briefly: manage.py is a wrapper around django-admin tied to a specific project. It sets the DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE environment variable pointing at your settings module and then hands off control. That's why commands like python manage.py run in the context of your particular project - with the right database, apps, and config. A handy roster of commands to mention: runserver, migrate, makemigrations, startapp, createsuperuser, shell.

cards.front: Why put DRF on top of Django, and what does it handle for you?
cards.back: DRF is a toolkit for REST APIs on top of Django. It frees you from boilerplate code: serializers translate models into JSON and back, class-based views handle the HTTP methods, and authentication mechanisms (tokens, JWT, OAuth) and permissions decide who can do what. I reach for DRF when I need an API for an SPA, a mobile client, or an external integration, and I can override almost any of its components for the project when needed.

cards.front: Signals in Django: how to react to events without tight coupling?
cards.back: Signals provide loose coupling: the sender of an event doesn't know who will react to it or how. For example, on the post_save of the Article model I attach a receiver that notifies subscribers, and the article-saving code stays clean. The upsides are separation of concerns and testability. But don't overuse them: signals make the control flow implicit, so for logic that should be visible "right there in place" I prefer an explicit call in the save() method or in a service layer.

cards.front: Which base classes does a Django application rest on?
cards.back: The skeleton of any Django application rests on four pillars. Model describes the data structure - each class corresponds to a table, and the ORM generates the SQL itself. View is the layer that takes a request and returns a response; it's written as a function or a class subclassing django.views.View. Form validates and processes user input. And by registering a model in the admin, you get a CRUD interface with almost no code. These four cover the MTV pattern the framework is built on.

cards.front: What does Django give you out of the box for login and permission checks?
cards.back: Django covers both security concerns. Authentication (who you are) rests on the User model and backends that verify the username and password; passwords are stored as hashes, not plaintext. Authorization (what you're allowed to do) is built on groups and permissions. The ready-made LoginView and LogoutView handle login and logout, and the @login_required decorator or the LoginRequiredMixin mixin lock views away from anonymous users. The session mechanism carries the user's state between requests, so there's no need to log in again on every page. If the standard fields aren't enough, you create a custom model via AUTH_USER_MODEL.

cards.front: In what ways can one Django model inherit from another?
cards.back: There are three inheritance options. Abstract models (abstract = True in Meta) are a pure field template: the base class has no table, the fields are copied into each child model, and only the descendants get their own table. Multi-table inheritance is when the base class is not abstract: it has its own table, the descendant has its own, with an implicit OneToOne relation between them, and accessing an inherited field triggers a JOIN. A proxy model (proxy = True) is the same table but a different Python class: you can add methods, change the manager, or alter the default ordering, without a single new column. The choice comes down to one question: do you need a separate table, and do you need new fields?

cards.front: When to use @task and when @shared_task to declare Celery tasks?
cards.back: Both decorators register a function as an asynchronous task. The difference is in the binding to the Celery instance. @task is taken straight off a specific app object and registers the task in that one, so it fits when the task lives inside a single project and doesn't need to be reused. @shared_task returns a proxy that finds the right application at call time rather than at declaration time. That's critical for libraries and Django apps that get plugged into different projects: at import time a specific app may not exist yet. The practical rule: inside a reusable app or package always use @shared_task; for tasks hard-bound to a single app you can use @task.

cards.front: What are the main building blocks Django is divided into?
cards.back: Django is built on the MTV scheme: the Model is responsible for data, the Template for display, and the View ties them together and holds the business logic. A model describes the structure of the data, and usually one model maps to one table; the ORM handles talking to the database, translating Python objects into SQL. A view receives a request and returns a response: HTML, JSON, or a redirect. Templates written in DTL assemble HTML and keep the markup separate from the logic. Routing (urls.py) maps addresses to views. On top of this, Django automatically builds an admin panel from the models. It is important not to mix up the terminology: what MVC calls the "controller" is called a View in Django, and the Template is the presentation layer.

cards.front: Transactions in Django ORM: how to ensure atomicity of database operations
cards.back: A transaction guarantees that a group of changes to the database is either committed in full or fully canceled on error - so the data is never left in a half-applied state. By default Django wraps each HTTP request in a transaction (the ATOMIC_REQUESTS mode), but for fine-grained control I use transaction.atomic: either as a decorator on a function or as a with block. Inside the block, any unhandled exception rolls back all changes. Nested atomic blocks create savepoints, which lets you roll back only part of the operations.

cards.front: Lazy vs eager loading in Django ORM: select_related and prefetch_related
cards.back: By default the ORM is lazy: Model.objects.all() does not hit the database immediately but returns a QuerySet that is executed only on iteration, slicing, or list(). This is convenient, but when traversing related objects the N+1 problem is born: for each object - a separate query for its relation. The cure is eager loading. select_related() does a SQL JOIN and pulls "to-one" relations (ForeignKey, OneToOne) in a single query. prefetch_related() fetches "to-many" relations (ManyToMany, reverse FK) with a separate query and stitches the data together on the Python side. The choice depends on the type of relation.

cards.front: DRF serializers: a two-way bridge between Python objects and JSON
cards.back: A serializer solves two tasks. On the way out (serialization) it turns a Django object into a dictionary of primitives, which the DRF renderer hands to the client as JSON. On the way in (deserialization) it accepts raw client data, runs it through is_valid(), and on success places the clean data into validated_data, from where create() or update() pick it up. The key point: invalid data never reaches the model - validation is built into the serializer itself. Reading an object goes through serializer.data, writing goes through serializer(data=...) and is_valid().

cards.front: ModelSerializer or Serializer in DRF: when auto-generated fields save code
cards.back: Both turn objects into JSON and back, but they differ in the amount of manual work. With a Serializer I declare every field explicitly and write create()/update() myself - maximum control, but also maximum code. A ModelSerializer, through a class Meta with model and fields, generates fields from the model's metadata and implements saving automatically - less code, fewer mistakes. In practice, for a model I almost always reach for ModelSerializer, and I turn to a bare Serializer when the data is not tied to a single model or a non-standard structure is needed. The auto fields of ModelSerializer can still be overridden.

cards.front: What tools does Django use to speed up responses through caching?
cards.back: Caching in Django works at three altitudes. The @cache_page decorator wraps a view and serves the ready-made HTML until the timeout expires. The {% cache %} template tag caches only a fragment - for example, a shared menu. And when flexibility is needed, I reach for the low-level API django.core.cache.cache with its set/get methods and decide myself what to store and for how long. The backend (Redis, Memcached, local memory) is configured with a single CACHES section in settings.py, so the caching code does not depend on the choice of store.

cards.front: How does fetching with get() differ from filter() and exclude()?
cards.back: The key difference is what the method returns and how it reacts to "the wrong count". get() is designed strictly for one record: zero matches yields DoesNotExist, two or more yields MultipleObjectsReturned. That is why it is used only on unique fields (pk, id, a unique field). filter() and exclude() return a lazy QuerySet and throw no exceptions: when there are no matches they simply return an empty set. filter selects records that satisfy the condition, exclude returns the logically opposite set. In practice this is WHERE condition versus WHERE NOT condition.

cards.front: What problem do custom model managers and QuerySet methods solve?
cards.back: This is a way to avoid smearing the same filter(status='published') all over the project. I encapsulate such logic in the model: either I override the manager (for example, to return only published items by default through get_queryset), or I create a QuerySet class with methods like published() and attach it via as_manager(). The main advantage of the second approach is that the methods stay chainable: Article.objects.published().filter(...). As a result the views become shorter and more readable, while the selection rules live in one place and are tested in isolation.

cards.front: When to use APIView vs ViewSet in DRF?
cards.back: I choose based on whether the endpoint fits standard CRUD. If the API is resource-oriented — list, create, read, update, delete — I use ModelViewSet plus a router: a couple of lines, and routes with all methods are generated automatically. If I need non-standard logic, custom actions, unusual methods, or the endpoint isn't really about a "resource," I use APIView and write get()/post() explicitly. Essentially, a ViewSet is a grouping of several views with automatic routing built on the same mechanism as APIView.

cards.front: Django's CSRF token mechanism: how the framework blocks forged requests
cards.back: Django requires that any unsafe HTTP request (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) carry a valid CSRF token, otherwise CsrfViewMiddleware rejects it with a 403 response. The token is tied to a specific user and checked for a match. Since the attacker's site has no access to the contents of your page and can't read the token value, it physically can't form a valid request — even though the browser supplies the session cookie itself. The key idea: cookies alone aren't enough to confirm an action; you need a secret that a third-party domain can't access.

cards.front: select_related or prefetch_related: which tool fixes N+1, and when?
cards.back: select_related is eager loading via a JOIN: the related objects arrive in the same query as the main ones. It applies to relations where there's one related record per row. prefetch_related runs an additional query against the related table and joins the data on the Python side; it applies to multiple relations, where a JOIN would bloat the result with duplicates. The main goal of both is to eliminate the N+1 problem: when, instead of one query, the ORM makes a query on every iteration of a loop.

cards.front: How does annotate differ from aggregate in the Django ORM, and which to use when?
cards.back: annotate returns a QuerySet with an added field and stays lazy: the query hasn't run yet, and you can filter and order it further. aggregate is a terminal operation: it hits the database immediately and returns a dict. The main benefit of both is that summing and grouping happen on the database side (via GROUP BY and aggregate SQL functions), not in Python after pulling all the rows into memory.

cards.front: When to pull dictionaries from the ORM via values(), and when tuples via values_list()?
cards.back: values() and values_list() turn a QuerySet of model objects into a lightweight dataset. I use values() when it's more convenient to access fields by name (for example, to hand the data straight to JSON or a template), and values_list() when the names don't matter and I only need the values themselves. To select a single column I add flat=True — I get a flat list instead of a list of single-element tuples. The main motivation is to avoid dragging extra fields out of the database and instantiating heavy model objects where they aren't needed.

cards.front: How do you control access to endpoints in DRF through permission classes?
cards.back: DRF factors access checks out into separate permission classes, which are listed in the view's permission_classes attribute. For every request the framework runs them one by one; if even one returns False, the client gets a 403 (or 401, if they aren't authenticated at all). Out of the box there are AllowAny, IsAuthenticated, IsAdminUser, IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly. For non-standard logic I subclass BasePermission and override has_permission (access to the resource as a whole) or has_object_permission (access to a specific object). This way the access rules live separately from the view's business logic.

cards.front: How does a DRF serializer validate input data at two levels of validation?
cards.back: Validation is triggered by calling serializer.is_valid(). DRF first runs the built-in type and field-constraint checks (EmailField, max_length, etc.), then my validate_ methods for individual fields, and finally the validate(self, data) method for cross-field rules. Each method either returns a (possibly modified) value or raises serializers.ValidationError. All the collected errors end up in serializer.errors. Importantly, validate(self, data) only sees the fields that passed the previous stages.

cards.front: Why can't async views in Django be considered a "free" speedup?
cards.back: Django can do async def views, but the framework is historically synchronous, and many of its parts still are. If you directly call blocking synchronous code from an async view — for example, a synchronous ORM call — it will stall the event loop and kill the point of asynchrony. That's why synchronous sections are wrapped in sync_to_async from asgiref. On top of that, not every middleware and not every third-party library is async: Django can "adapt" them, but through a context switch, which adds overhead. An async view is justified for I/O-bound load (external APIs, files), and for CPU-bound tasks it's useless.

cards.front: How do you organize business-logic layers in a Django project?
cards.back: State the principle and list the layers where logic goes: 1. **Models** - methods that compute and validate on top of an object's own data ("fat models"). 2. **Managers and QuerySets** - encapsulate common database queries so you don't duplicate filters all over the codebase. 3. **Services and utilities** - operations spanning multiple models or external systems; not tied to a single model. 4. **Views** - stay thin: parse the request, call a service/model, return the response. 5. **Signals** - reacting to events (for example, an action after save), but without overuse: implicit links make debugging harder. The main argument is separation of concerns: each layer is easier to test and reuse.

cards.front: select_related vs prefetch_related: one JOIN or a second query
cards.back: "The difference is who joins the data. select_related offloads the work to the database: one JOIN, one query, suitable for relations where exactly one object sits on one side - that is, ForeignKey and OneToOneField. prefetch_related runs two queries and joins the results on the Python side by a key field; you reach for it with ManyToManyField and reverse FK, where a JOIN would inflate the result set with duplicates. In practice I first catch the N+1, then pick the method by the relation type, and for nested relations I combine both and use Prefetch to fine-tune the nested query."

cards.front: How Docker differs from Docker Compose and where the line between them lies
cards.back: Docker is a containerization engine: it takes an application with all its dependencies and packages it into a container that behaves identically on any machine running Docker. Docker Compose is a tool on top of Docker for multi-container applications: instead of manually starting each container, I describe all services, networks, and volumes in docker-compose.yml and bring them up with a single docker compose up command. Roughly: Docker is about one container, Compose is about managing a group of them as a single application.

cards.front: merge or rebase: two ways to combine branches and what each costs
cards.back: Both commands move changes between branches, but they produce different histories. merge creates a separate merge commit with two parents and keeps the branch points visible - the whole picture is preserved. rebase lifts the current branch's commits and replays them on top of another branch's tip; history becomes linear, but commit hashes change. That's why rebase must not be applied to branches that are already published and visible to others: rewritten history will break their work. Linearity is convenient to read, branchiness more honestly reflects how development actually went.

cards.front: cherry-pick: how to move a single commit without dragging the whole branch along
cards.back: cherry-pick is needed when you require exactly one change from someone else's branch, not its whole history. The classic case: a bug was found and fixed in an experimental branch, and the fix is urgently needed in main. Instead of merging the whole branch, we take only the needed commit by its hash - Git creates a new commit in the current branch with the same changes but a new hash. The downside is that careless use leaves duplicates of the same change in history, and when edits overlap you'll have to resolve a conflict manually.

cards.front: Git Flow: branching discipline with long-lived and temporary branches
cards.back: Git Flow is a branching model proposed by Vincent Driessen. At its core are two long-lived branches: main with stable release code and develop as the integration branch where finished features converge. On top of them live temporary branches: feature branches grow out of develop and return into it; release branches split off from develop for final polishing and merge into main and develop; hotfix branches come from main for urgent fixes and also return into both. The model gives a predictable process and fits large teams and scheduled releases well, but for frequent deployments it's too heavy - there, a trunk-based approach is more often chosen.

cards.front: The minimal working set of Git commands and what each one does
cards.back: Git stores the history of changes and lets you work on code in parallel.
