From the daily minimum I'd name: git init creates a repository, git clone copies an existing one, git add stages changes, git commit records them as a snapshot, git status shows the state, git push sends commits to the server, git pull fetches others' changes, git branch and git checkout manage branches, git merge joins them, and git log shows the history. The key idea is the three-stage model: working directory, index (staging), and commit history; add moves from the first to the second, commit from the second to the third.

cards.front: The minimal set of Docker commands you can't do without in practice
cards.back: I'd break the commands down by lifecycle stages. Building and delivering images: docker build builds an image from a Dockerfile, docker pull pulls a ready one from a registry, docker push sends yours back. Running and managing containers: docker run creates and starts a container, docker ps shows running ones (with -a — all of them), docker stop/docker start stop and bring them up. Debugging: docker exec -it bash opens a shell inside a live container. Cleanup: docker rm removes a container, docker rmi — an image.

cards.front: Why do you need git push --force and why is it dangerous?
cards.back: Force push (git push --force) sends a local branch to the server, ignoring the "remote history is an ancestor of the local one" check. You need it after rewriting history: rebase, commit --amend, squash, or removing an accidentally committed secret. A normal push is rejected in these cases because the branches have diverged. The main danger - you can wipe out commits someone else has already pushed. That's why in practice you almost always use git push --force-with-lease: it refuses to overwrite the branch if something you haven't seen has appeared on the server.

cards.front: How do pre-commit hooks filter code before the commit even happens?
cards.back: Pre-commit is a Git client-side hook that fires at the moment of git commit, before the commit is written. It usually runs linters, formatters, fast tests, and secret scanners. A non-zero exit code from any hook cancels the commit. In practice, hooks are rarely written by hand: you install the pre-commit framework with a declarative .pre-commit-config.yaml listing ready-made checks. An important nuance: this is local protection, it can be bypassed with the --no-verify flag, so the same checks are duplicated in CI as a mandatory barrier.

cards.front: Static typing in CI: how to make the pipeline check types automatically?
cards.back: I add type annotations to the code and, in CI, a dedicated job that installs mypy and runs it over the project. The real value isn't the check itself but the fact that it's mandatory and automatic: a developer can't "forget" to run the analyzer locally, because a red pipeline blocks the merge. This catches a whole class of errors (wrong argument type, a None where an object was expected) before the code runs and before code review, saving the reviewer's time.

cards.front: Linux: which commands show what's currently running on the system?
cards.back: If you need a one-off list of all processes with their owners and resource usage - ps aux, often paired with grep to find a specific process. If you need to watch the load over time (who's eating CPU and memory right now) - top or htop. The difference is fundamental: ps prints a single frame and exits, while top/htop are interactive monitors that refresh the picture every couple of seconds and let you sort processes or kill the one you want on the spot.

cards.front: Signals in Linux: what are they for and which ones are worth knowing by heart?
cards.back: Signals are an inter-process communication mechanism for short notifications. The key thing to say: most signals can be intercepted and handled by your own handler (for example, on SIGTERM you cleanly close connections and save data), but two signals - SIGKILL (9) and SIGSTOP - cannot be intercepted, ignored, or blocked; the kernel itself handles them. That's why the correct order for stopping a service is SIGTERM first, giving it time for a graceful shutdown, and only if that doesn't help - SIGKILL.

cards.front: What turns a Dockerfile into a finished image during a build
cards.back: The build happens in two steps. First I describe the recipe in a Dockerfile: which base image to start from, what to copy, which dependencies to install, what to run the application with. Then I run docker build -t myimage:latest ., where -t sets the name and tag, and . is the build context (the directory whose files are available to commands like COPY). Docker reads the instructions top to bottom, creates a layer for each, caches unchanged layers, and assembles them into the final image, from which containers are later spun up.

cards.front: Networking in Docker: what comes up on its own and what you have to set up by hand
cards.back: Out of the box a network already exists: on installation Docker creates several networks, and a container without an explicit --network lands in the default bridge. That's enough for simple cases - containers on the host can see each other and reach the internet. But in real projects I almost always create my own network via docker network create: it provides isolation of container groups and, crucially, built-in DNS - containers address each other by service name rather than by IP. So the answer is: the basic network appears automatically, but a meaningful topology you set up by hand.

cards.front: Container vs. virtual machine: where does the isolation boundary run?
cards.back: A virtual machine virtualizes the hardware: a hypervisor runs several guest OSes, each with its own kernel. A container virtualizes at the OS level: all containers use the host kernel, and the kernel itself separates them via namespaces and cgroups. Everything else follows from this: a container starts in milliseconds and weighs megabytes, a VM boots in seconds and drags along gigabytes of guest system. On the other hand, a VM gives a stronger security boundary and lets you run an OS with a different kernel (a Windows guest on a Linux host). A container is bound to the host kernel: on a Linux host, only Linux containers run natively.

cards.front: Docker daemon: who actually runs the containers?
cards.back: Docker is built on a client-server model. When you type docker run, the docker binary is just a client: it doesn't run the container itself but sends an HTTP request to the dockerd daemon over a socket. The daemon is the server that holds the state, talks to image registries, manages networks and volumes, and through the underlying runtime (containerd → runc) actually spins up the container processes. So if the daemon is stopped, no docker command will work, even though the client itself is installed and available.

cards.front: Layers and the build cache: why instruction order in a Dockerfile decides everything
cards.back: An image is a stack of layers, one layer per instruction (FROM, COPY, RUN). Docker computes a cache key for each instruction: for RUN it's the command text itself, for COPY/ADD it's also the checksum of the copied files. If the key matches the previous build - the layer is reused. The important point: the cache is chained. If layer N changes, N and every layer after it are invalidated, because each subsequent layer is built on top of the previous one. Hence the practical rule: place instructions from the least frequently changing to the most frequent. First copy the dependency manifest and install them, and only then copy the source code, which gets edited on every commit.

cards.front: Docker volumes: surviving container removal without losing data
cards.back: A container's writable layer is ephemeral: remove the container and everything it wrote to its filesystem is gone. For data that must outlive the container (databases, uploads, logs), you use a volume. It is Docker-managed storage: its lifecycle is not tied to the container, it can be reattached to a new container after a rebuild, and it can be shared between several. A volume differs from a bind mount in that Docker itself is responsible for where it lives and how it is managed (docker volume create/ls/rm), whereas a bind mount is hard-wired to a specific path on the host. For production data, a volume is almost always preferable.

cards.front: What does the operating system store about each process?
cards.back: A process is a running instance of a program, and the OS describes it with a Process Control Block structure. The minimal set interviewers expect to hear: the PID as a unique identifier; the state (running, ready, waiting, terminated); priority for the scheduler; the saved context (registers, instruction pointer, stack pointer) — which lets the process be paused and resumed from the same spot; the parent's PID, defining the hierarchy; the file descriptor table; information about allocated memory (code, data, stack, heap); accumulated CPU time. It is exactly the context that makes preemptive multitasking possible: when switching, the OS saves one process's context and restores another's.

cards.front: Why kill exists and why it is not only about killing processes
cards.back: kill sends a signal to a process by its identifier (PID). A signal is a number that the process reacts to with a predefined action. The two termination variants used most often: SIGTERM (15) — a polite request to shut down, which the process can intercept and clean up gracefully, and SIGKILL (9) — a forced removal that can be neither intercepted nor ignored, because the kernel itself handles it. The right order is SIGTERM first, and only SIGKILL if the process hangs.

cards.front: Zombie versus daemon: two completely different 'undead' in Linux
cards.back: A zombie (defunct) appears when a child process has finished and terminated, but the parent has not yet called wait()/waitpid() to read its return code. The code itself is no longer executing, the memory is freed — all that remains is a row in the process table with the exit code. A zombie cannot be "killed" with a signal, because there is nothing left to kill; it disappears when the parent calls wait(), and if the parent dies first, the zombie is adopted by init/systemd, which cleans up after it. A daemon is a specially crafted background process: it is detached from the controlling terminal via setsid(), and it runs independently of user sessions, serving the network, hardware, or periodic tasks.

cards.front: What systemd and sysfs are responsible for in modern Linux
cards.back: systemd and sysfs solve different problems and do not compete. systemd is responsible for starting and managing services: parallel startup with dependency awareness, restarting crashed processes, a single systemctl interface. It replaced System V init. sysfs is not about services but about access to the kernel's internals: through files in /sys you can read or change parameters of hardware and drivers. Roughly speaking, systemd manages what gets started, and sysfs shows what it runs on.

cards.front: Hard and symbolic links: how they differ at the inode level
cards.back: The difference is in what the link points to. A symbolic link points to a path — it is a tiny file with the route to the target written inside. Delete the original and the symlink dangles in the air (a "broken" link). A hard link points not to a path but directly at the inode, that is, at the very structure holding the file's data. So the original name and the hardlink are equals: deleting one of them does not touch the data until the inode's link count reaches zero. A symlink can reference directories and cross filesystem boundaries; a hardlink cannot.

cards.front: CMD versus ENTRYPOINT: who sets the command and who only the arguments?
cards.back: CMD is the default behavior: anything I pass to docker run replaces it entirely. ENTRYPOINT sets a fixed command, and arguments from docker run are merely appended to it. That is why a container with an ENTRYPOINT behaves like a standalone executable. In practice they are combined: ENTRYPOINT is the program itself, CMD is its default arguments, which can be overridden without touching the program.

cards.front: How to shrink a Docker image: working techniques against bloat
cards.back: I start with the base image: instead of ubuntu/debian I take alpine or a slim variant. Next I watch the layers — combining install and cleanup commands into a single RUN, so that cache and temporary files do not settle into a separate layer. For compiled languages I use a multi-stage build: in the first stage I build the binary with the full toolchain, in the second I copy only the finished artifact. On top of that I install packages with --no-install-recommends and do not drag dev dependencies into the final image.

cards.front: Docker network drivers: bridge, host, none, overlay - which to choose
cards.back: By default a container lands on the bridge - a virtual bridge on the host where containers see each other but reach the outside world only through port publishing. host gives the container the host's network stack with no separate namespace - faster, but with no isolation and no port publishing. none disables networking entirely for tasks that don't need it. overlay ties containers from different machines into one logical network - this is the mode for Swarm and clusters. The choice is a trade-off between isolation, performance, and scale.

cards.front: Logging in Docker: stdout, logging drivers, and centralized collection
cards.back: An application in a container should not write to files itself - it writes to the standard streams, and Docker captures them. From there everything is determined by the logging driver: by default it's json-file, which stores logs on the host as JSON, and you must give it rotation via max-size/max-file, otherwise the disk fills up. I view logs with docker logs, and in production I switch the driver to fluentd/syslog or collect everything into ELK for centralized search and analysis.

cards.front: What data model is Git's versioning built on?
cards.back: Git is built on a snapshot model: each commit stores the full tree of the project's state at the moment of recording, plus a reference to the parent commit. Unchanged files are not duplicated - Git reuses the previous object by content, so storage is economical. History is a directed graph of commits; a branch is just a movable pointer to a commit. A merge combines two lines of development, and a conflict arises when the same lines were edited differently and Git cannot pick automatically. Being distributed means every developer has a full copy of the history, not just the current slice.

cards.front: What container operations tasks does Kubernetes take off your plate
cards.back: Kubernetes covers the routine of operating distributed containerized applications. List point by point what exactly it takes on: **Declarative deployment.** You describe the result (a manifest), not a sequence of commands. The platform brings the cluster to that state and holds it there. **Scaling.** The number of instances adjusts to load - manually or automatically based on metrics. **Self-healing.** A crashed container is restarted, an unhealthy pod is replaced. The failure of a single instance doesn't take the application down. **Load balancing.** Incoming traffic is distributed evenly across live instances via a Service. **Configuration and secrets.** ConfigMap and Secret separate settings and sensitive data from the image. **Zero-downtime updates.** A rolling update replaces pods gradually, checking the readiness of new ones before shutting down the old ones. One sentence to sum up: Kubernetes turns the manual operation of containers into managing a desired state through a continuous reconciliation loop.

cards.front: The process lifecycle in Linux: PID, states, and the scheduler
cards.back: Process management is a core kernel function. A process is a running instance of a program with its own address space, code, data, and PID identifier. Through the scheduler the kernel distributes CPU among processes by a chosen algorithm, and the processes themselves move between states: running on a core, waiting for I/O, suspended, or having become a zombie after termination. Creation happens via fork(), replacing the image via exec(), synchronizing with a child via wait(). At the command level, ps, top, and kill stand behind observation and control.

cards.front: Virtual filesystems vs device files: the roles of /proc, /sys, and /dev
cards.back: All three directories look like ordinary folders, but they differ behind the scenes. /proc is a virtual filesystem with information about processes (/proc/ ) and the system (/proc/cpuinfo, /proc/meminfo), plus kernel parameters tunable on the fly in /proc/sys. /sys is also a virtual filesystem, but it's a structured interface to devices and drivers through the kernel model (sysfs). And /dev is device nodes: block (/dev/sda), character (/dev/tty), and pseudo-devices (/dev/null), through which actual I/O to the hardware goes. In short: /proc and /sys are for reading and tuning, /dev is for accessing the devices themselves.

cards.front: Poetry as a dependency manager: how it beats the pip + setuptools combo
cards.back: Poetry is a tool that unifies dependency management and Python package building into a single workflow. Its main advantages over pip/setuptools: it picks compatible versions itself (a real resolver, not sequential installation), keeps exact versions in poetry.lock for reproducibility, automatically creates and activates a virtual environment, and describes the project declaratively in pyproject.toml instead of a scattering of requirements.txt plus setup.py. It also takes building and publishing to PyPI on itself with ready-made commands. The result is less manual fiddling with versions and environments.

cards.front: Caching in the Grafana stack: where it lives and how it affects metric freshness
cards.back: Grafana is a visualization layer, not storage. Dashboards are built from data it queries from sources (Prometheus, InfluxDB, Graphite). So by default Grafana caches nothing - every panel refresh goes to the source. Caching appears in three places: at the sources themselves (Prometheus keeps recent data in memory), at a caching proxy in front of the source (Nginx, Varnish), and through plugins/Enterprise features that store results in Redis or Memcached. Important: the cache affects only freshness - with too long a TTL the user sees stale points, so the lifetime is tuned to the freshness requirements.

cards.front: A process PID: what is this number and what role does it play in the OS?
cards.back: A PID is a process identifier issued by the kernel at the moment the process starts. It is unique among live processes and stays bound to the process until it terminates. When a process dies, its PID is released and may eventually be reused by a new process - so a PID can't be treated as a permanent reference. In practice, a PID is needed to address a process: to check its status via ps or /proc/ , to send a signal via kill, or to limit its resources. On Linux the first process (init/systemd) always has PID 1 and is the ancestor of all the others.

cards.front: The Linux file permission model: owner, group, others, and three flags
cards.back: Linux permissions are arranged as a "who × what's allowed" matrix. Horizontally there are three classes of subjects: the file's owner, its group, and everyone else. For each class three bits are defined - r, w, x. In ls -l output this is ten characters: the first is the object type, then three triplets in a row. I change permissions with chmod (symbolically u+x or numerically 755), the owner with chown, the group with chgrp. The numeric notation is convenient because each digit is a sum of bits: 4 (r) + 2 (w) + 1 (x).

cards.front: How to check RAM usage in Linux
cards.back: For a quick look I use free -h - it immediately shows total/used/free, and most importantly the available column, which is a realistic estimate of how much memory is available to new processes without swapping. If I need details on specific counters (Buffers, Cached, Slab, etc.), I look at /proc/meminfo - a procfs pseudo-file that the kernel updates on the fly. The key point: high used does not mean a shortage of memory - the page cache counts as "used" but is handed back to applications on demand, so you should look at available, not free.

cards.front: Checking whether a TCP port is reachable on a remote server
cards.back: For a one-off check I usually reach for nc -zv host port - it doesn't push any data, it just verifies the connection and prints the result, plus it can do UDP. telnet host port is a fallback when netcat isn't installed: if the connection establishes, the port is reachable. I use nmap -p port host when I need to scan a range of ports or also figure out which service sits behind the port. It's important to distinguish three outcomes: "open" (there's a response), "closed" (RST - the host is there but nobody's listening), and "filtered" (silence - a firewall is dropping traffic).

cards.front: A baseline set of commands for a Linux system administrator
cards.back: I'd group the commands by task rather than memorize a flat list. Privileges - sudo for a one-off elevation of rights. Users - useradd/usermod (for example, usermod -aG adds to a group without wiping the existing ones). File permissions - chmod and chown. Services on modern systems - via systemctl (start/stop/status/enable). Disk - df for partitions and du for specific directories. Processes - ps/top to view and kill to terminate by PID. The main thing in an interview is to show that you understand *why* a command exists, not just its syntax.

cards.front: How do you organize branching in git, and why is this discipline needed?
cards.back: I tailor the choice of workflow to the team and the release cadence. Vincent Driessen's classic Git Flow keeps the long-lived branches main and develop plus short-lived feature, release, and hotfix branches; it's good where you have versioned releases and several parallel streams of work. But if we ship to production several times a day, I prefer trunk-based or GitHub Flow: one main branch, short feature branches, and a quick merge via pull request with mandatory code review and a green CI. The main principle is the same: the main branch is always deployable, branches are short-lived, and the whole history stays readable. Git Flow by itself isn't an end in itself - it's a tool for a particular release rhythm.

cards.front: Decoding SOLID: the five pillars of maintainable OOP code
cards.back: SOLID is made up of five principles: single responsibility (a class changes for one reason), open/closed (we add behavior by extension, not by editing), Liskov substitution (a subclass works wherever the base class does without breaking the logic), interface segregation (a client does not depend on methods it doesn't need), and dependency inversion (modules depend on abstractions, not on concrete implementations). By following them, we get loosely coupled, easily extensible code.

cards.front: Polymorphism made simple: one call, many behaviors
cards.back: Polymorphism is the ability of code to work with objects of different types through a common interface: you call obj.speak(), and the concrete implementation is selected based on the object's actual type. In Python it is dynamic and relies on duck typing — what matters is not which class the object belongs to, but whether it has the needed method. Because of this, a function expecting "something that can speak" works the same with a dog, a cat, or any new class added later.

cards.front: Encapsulation: why data is hidden behind methods
cards.back: Encapsulation is hiding an object's internal structure behind an interface of methods, so that from the outside you can't corrupt its state by bypassing the checks. Python has no strict access modifiers like Java: everything rests on conventions. A single underscore _attr means "you shouldn't touch this from the outside"; two underscores __attr enable name mangling, and the attribute becomes _ClassName__attr. This doesn't make it truly private — it only makes accidental access harder and protects against name collisions in subclasses.

cards.front: Singleton: how to keep exactly one instance of a class
cards.back: Singleton is a creational pattern that restricts a class to exactly one instance and opens up global access to it. In Python it's usually done by overriding __new__: the first call creates the object and caches it in a class attribute, and subsequent calls return the same object. An important caveat: global state complicates testing and, in a multithreaded environment, requires a lock. Often, instead of the classic Singleton, Python developers use a module (which is already one per process) or dependency injection.

cards.front: What problem does super() solve, and why you shouldn't hardcode the parent's name
cards.back: super() returns a proxy object that delegates the call to the next class in the current object's MRO, not to the "parent" in the naive sense. The main benefit is decoupling from a specific class name: when you rename or rearrange base classes, the code keeps working. In cooperative multiple inheritance, it's exactly super() that guarantees each class in a diamond scheme runs exactly once and the common ancestor isn't called twice.

cards.front: Who creates the object and who configures it: __new__ vs __init__
cards.back: An object's birth in Python is two steps. First __new__(cls, ...) is called — a static method that allocates and returns the instance. Then Python calls __init__(self, ...) on that instance to set its attributes. The key nuance: __init__ returns nothing (only None) and does not create the object, it merely configures it. If __new__ returns an instance of a different class, __init__ may not be called for it at all. You should touch __new__ only when you need to intervene precisely at the moment of creation — for example, with immutable tuple/str, where the value is fixed at creation time.

cards.front: Magic methods: how to teach your class to behave like a built-in type
cards.back: Magic methods are a protocol between your class and the interpreter. When I write a + b, Python doesn't "add objects" itself, it calls a.__add__(b); len(x) under the hood is x.__len__(); print(x) reaches for __str__. So by implementing the right dunder methods, I make the object compatible with operators, the container protocol, iteration and formatting — without special APIs, purely through familiar syntax. This is exactly "Pythonic-ness": code using my objects reads the same as code with lists and numbers.

cards.front: The __del__ destructor in Python: why it exists and why you can't rely on it
cards.back: __del__ is called when an object's reference count drops to zero and the garbage collector decides to remove it. The problem is that the moment of this event is non-deterministic: with circular references the object can live until the cyclic collector runs, and on program exit __del__ may not run at all. So for deterministic resource release — files, sockets, connections — Python uses not the destructor but context managers (with and the __enter__/__exit__ protocol) or an explicit close(). __del__ is left as a last-resort safety net, not as the main path.

cards.front: Visitor: how to add operations without cracking open object classes
cards.back: This is a behavioral pattern. It has two sides: a hierarchy of elements with an accept method and a hierarchy of visitors with a visit method for each element type. In accept, the element calls the appropriate visit on the visitor, passing self (this is called double dispatch). Thanks to this, adding a new operation means simply writing another visitor class rather than amending every element class. The price we pay is that a new element type breaks all existing visitors. So Visitor is appropriate when the set of types is stable but the operations over them change often.

cards.front: Template Method: fix the algorithm's skeleton, hand the steps to subclasses
cards.back: A behavioral pattern. The base class has a single public skeleton method that calls a set of smaller steps in the right order. Some steps are implemented in the base and shared by everyone, some are declared abstract or as a stub and overridden by subclasses. The client invokes only the skeleton method, while the internal content varies. The pattern removes duplication of the common part and at the same time prevents subclasses from breaking the order of steps — inversion of control in the spirit of "don't call us, we'll call you".

cards.front: Strategy: swapping an algorithm at runtime through composition
cards.back: A behavioral pattern built on composition, not inheritance. The context holds a reference to a strategy object and delegates the work to it through a single method. Any algorithm is a separate strategy with the same interface, so they can be swapped at runtime without touching the context. The main benefit is removing sprawling if/elif branches keyed on algorithm type and gaining openness for extension: a new variant means a new class. In Python a strategy is often just a function, since functions are first-class objects.

cards.front: State: an object changes behavior by delegating it to the current state
cards.back: A behavioral pattern. The context holds a reference to a state object and forwards calls to it. All states share a common interface, and the transitions themselves (who follows whom) are defined inside the states. This removes bulky conditionals checking "what mode am I in right now" and makes adding a new state a local change - a new class. Structurally it resembles Strategy, but the meaning is different: State models a finite state machine and manages the transitions itself, whereas Strategy simply swaps the algorithm at the client's will.

cards.front: Observer: how objects subscribe to changes in another object's state
cards.back: Observer solves the problem: how to notify a set of objects about a change in another object's state without tightly coupling them. There is a publisher (Subject) that keeps a list of subscribers (Observers) and, on every change, walks the list and calls an agreed method like update on each one. The subscribers react on their own. The source depends not on concrete classes but on a common observer interface, so receivers can be added and removed on the fly without touching the publisher's code. Classic use cases are GUIs, event buses, and the Model layer in MVC.

cards.front: Types of inheritance in Python: from single to hybrid
cards.back: Five schemes are distinguished. **Single** - one parent. **Multiple** - several parents at once for a single class. **Multilevel** - a chain A → B → C, where a descendant inherits from a descendant. **Hierarchical** - one parent with several direct descendants. **Hybrid** - a combination of the previous ones, for example a diamond scheme where two ancestors converge in a common forebear. Python supports multiple inheritance, and the order in which attributes and methods are looked up across this class graph is determined by the MRO (Method Resolution Order), computed by the C3 algorithm.

cards.front: The __init__ method: what initializes an object and why it is needed
cards.back: __init__ is a special class method that runs when each instance is created. As its first argument it receives the new object itself (self), followed by any parameters for configuration. Inside, we attach attributes onto self. Strictly speaking, the object is created by __new__ (it allocates memory and returns the instance), while __init__ only fills an already-ready object with data and returns nothing. That is why in everyday speech __init__ is called a constructor, even though construction is the pair __new__ + __init__.

cards.front: The __call__ method: how to make an instance a callable object
cards.back: In Python, callability is simply the presence of a __call__ method. Every function is callable because it has this method; conversely, an ordinary object becomes callable if you define __call__ in its class. Python unfolds obj(5) into type(obj).__call__(obj, 5). This is handy for function-objects that hold configuration or accumulated state: function factories, decorator classes, configurable predicates, models that you "call" to get a prediction. You can check callability with callable(obj).

cards.front: How do you roll an object back to a past state without exposing its internals?
cards.back: Memento is a behavioral pattern that solves the problem of undoing actions while preserving encapsulation. I distinguish three roles: the Originator holds the data and itself knows how to create and accept a snapshot; the Memento is an immutable state container that does not reveal its internals to the outside; the Caretaker keeps a history of snapshots but does not poke into their contents. When a rollback is needed, the Caretaker hands the Originator the required snapshot, and the Originator restores itself. The key idea: only the Originator knows how to read and apply a snapshot, so encapsulation is not broken.

cards.front: How do you decouple a pile of objects that all hold direct references to each other?
cards.back: Mediator is a behavioral pattern that fights tight coupling. The problem: when N components talk directly, the number of links grows quadratically, and any change drags edits through all the neighbors. The mediator introduces a single object that encapsulates the interaction protocol. Components no longer know about each other - they only know the mediator and send it notifications about events. The mediator decides whom to nudge and how in response. This lowers coupling and makes the interaction logic centralized and testable. The classic analogy is an airport controller: pilots do not talk to each other, everything goes through the tower.

cards.front: How do you describe your own mini-language and evaluate expressions by its grammar?
cards.back: Interpreter is a behavioral pattern for the task of "parse and evaluate an expression according to a given grammar." Each grammar rule corresponds to its own expression class with a shared interpret(context) interface. Terminal expressions are leaves (a number, a variable), non-terminal ones are composite nodes (addition, multiplication) that recursively call interpret on their children. The context carries shared state, for example variable values. An expression tree is assembled from these nodes, and a single interpret call on the root launches the recursion downward. I use it when a small custom language is needed; for serious languages I take a ready-made parser, because the number of classes per rule becomes too large.

cards.front: How do you turn an action call into an object so it can be stored and undone?
cards.back: Command is a behavioral pattern that reifies a request. Instead of calling receiver.do() directly, I create a command object that seals everything needed for the action: a reference to the executor and the parameters. Roles emerge: the client creates the command, the invoker stores and runs it through a unified execute, the receiver does the real work. This gives four benefits: decoupling the initiator from the executor, easily adding new commands without touching old code, undo/redo (the command stores the data for undoing), and assembling macro-commands from simple ones. Classic uses are buttons and hotkeys in a UI, task queues, transactions.

cards.front: How a request travels through a line of handlers (Chain of Responsibility)
cards.back: Chain of Responsibility is a behavioral pattern. I arrange the handlers into a linked list where each one has a reference to the next. The request walks down the chain until someone accepts it. This decouples the sender from the receiver: I can add, remove, or reorder links without touching the client code. Classic uses are a validation pipeline, multi-level logging, and event handling in a UI.

cards.front: Patterns that manage object interaction (behavioral)
cards.back: This is one of the three groups of GoF patterns, alongside creational and structural ones. Creational patterns handle object creation, structural ones handle their composition, and behavioral ones handle communication and the distribution of responsibilities at runtime. They reduce coupling: objects communicate through abstractions rather than directly. Among the most common, I'd name Observer, Strategy, and Command.

cards.front: A stand-in object in front of the real object (the Proxy pattern)
cards.back: Proxy is a structural pattern. The surrogate implements the same interface as the real object and holds a reference to it. On a method call it can do something before or after delegating: check permissions, return a cached result, defer the expensive creation of an object, or write a log. To the client, the surrogate and the real object are indistinguishable — that's the whole point.

cards.front: How to reuse shared state across many objects (the Flyweight pattern)
cards.back: Flyweight is a structural pattern for cases where thousands or millions of objects differ only in a small part of their data. I extract the immutable shared part (intrinsic state) into one flyweight object and share it across all of them, while the mutable unique part (extrinsic state) is not stored inside but passed as an argument at use time. A factory with a cache guarantees that only one flyweight is created per identical intrinsic state.

cards.front: Why wrap a complex subsystem behind a single Facade class
cards.back: A facade is a structural pattern. I introduce a separate class that aggregates the needed subsystem components and exposes a narrow, convenient interface tailored to the client's scenarios. It doesn't change the internal logic of the components - it only frees the calling side from having to know the order of calls and the dependencies between them. This reduces coupling: the client depends on the facade rather than on a dozen details.

cards.front: How to treat an element and a group uniformly via the Composite
cards.back: The Composite is a structural pattern for tree-shaped hierarchies. I define a common component interface that both the leaf (the terminal element) and the container (which holds other components) inherit from. The container implements the operation by delegating it to its children recursively. Thanks to the shared interface, client code is identical for a single object and for an entire subtree - this removes the "if it's a group, then..." branching.

cards.front: Why the Bridge separates abstraction and implementation into different hierarchies
cards.back: The Bridge is a structural pattern. When there are two independent dimensions of variation (say, the shape type and the rendering platform), inheritance would force you to create a class for every pair - the count grows as a product. I move one dimension into an abstraction hierarchy and the other into an implementation hierarchy, and connect them through composition: the abstraction holds a reference to an implementation object and delegates the low-level calls to it. Now both dimensions can be extended independently, without touching each other's code.

cards.front: How the Adapter reconciles incompatible interfaces without changing their code
cards.back: The Adapter is a structural pattern. When I have a ready-made class with one interface but the client expects another, I rewrite neither. Instead I create a wrapper class that implements the expected interface and internally holds a reference to the adaptee, translating calls into its methods. This is a lifesaver when the original code can't be touched - a third-party library, legacy code, or a changed external API.

cards.front: Why structural patterns exist and what problems they solve
cards.back: This is one of the three GoF pattern families (alongside creational and behavioral). Structural patterns describe how to assemble objects into composite structures: Adapter bridges incompatible interfaces, Composite lets you treat a tree of objects as a single element, and Facade hides a complex subsystem behind a simple entry point. Their main goal is to reduce coupling and simplify maintenance without changing the behavior of the individual parts.

cards.front: Prototype: when cloning a new object beats building it from scratch
cards.back: Prototype gives objects a cloning method (usually clone()), and new instances are produced by copying existing ones. This saves on complex initialization and makes it easy to spawn objects with a similar configuration. In Python the copy module is the canonical tool: copy.copy makes a shallow copy, copy.deepcopy makes a deep one. The key interview nuance is the difference between them: with a shallow copy, nested mutable objects remain shared.

cards.front: Factory Method: how to delegate the choice of class to subclasses
cards.back: Instead of calling the constructor directly, the code calls a factory method that returns an object of the required type. Subclasses override this method and substitute the class being created. The benefit is loose coupling and extensibility: a new type is added as a separate subclass, while the consumer stays untouched (hello, Open/Closed principle). It is important not to confuse the "factory method" with a plain "switch factory": the classic pattern moves the choice specifically into an overridable subclass method.

cards.front: Builder: assembling a complex object step by step without a monster constructor
cards.back: Builder rescues you from the "telescoping constructor" - a long list of optional arguments that is impossible to read. Instead, the object is assembled in steps: individual methods set the parts, and build() returns the finished product. The methods often return self, which gives a fluent interface (method chaining). Optionally there is a Director - it encapsulates a typical order of steps so the client does not repeat the assembly recipe.

cards.front: A factory of object families: how Abstract Factory works
cards.back: The abstract factory defines an interface for creating a group of interrelated products so that client code does not depend on concrete classes. Each concrete factory produces its own consistent set of products (for example, everything for Windows or everything for macOS), while the client works only through abstractions. This makes it easy to swap an entire family at once and protects against mixing incompatible elements.

cards.front: Calling base class methods from a subclass: why super() is needed
cards.back: From a subclass method you reach the parent through super(). Most often it is called in __init__, so that the parent initializes its part of the state and the subclass adds its own. super() does not simply "go to the parent" - it follows the method resolution order (MRO), so it works correctly even with multiple inheritance, visiting each class exactly once.

cards.front: Three ways to declare a class in Python: class, type(), and metaclasses
cards.back: The standard path is the class syntax. It is just syntactic sugar: the interpreter still calls the metaclass (by default type), passing it the name, a tuple of bases, and the namespace dictionary. This means a class can be created by hand through type('Name', (Base,), {...}) for dynamic generation, and if you need to intervene at the moment any class is created, you attach a metaclass by overriding __new__ or __init__.

cards.front: Which OOP principles Python supports and how they look in practice
cards.back: Name the four classic pillars - encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction - and add composition as a practical technique. In Python polymorphism is largely built on duck typing: what matters is not inheritance but the presence of the required method. Abstraction is defined through the abc module, while encapsulation rests on conventions (_, __) rather than strict hiding.

cards.front: Why a class needs multiple parents and what you pay for it
cards.back: Multiple inheritance is listing several base classes in the parentheses when declaring a class. The main risk here is the diamond hierarchy: two parents share a common ancestor, and without rules it would be unclear which method to call. Python resolves this deterministically through C3 linearization, which builds a linear list of classes (the MRO). Attribute lookup goes through this list from left to right, and the first one that has the required method wins. You can view the order through ClassName.mro().

cards.front: How Python decides whose method to call when there are many parents
cards.back: The MRO answers the question "in what order does Python walk classes when looking for an attribute." For each class this order is computed once when the class is created, using the C3 algorithm, and stored in __mro__. C3 gives three guarantees: a class comes before its ancestors, locally closer classes take priority over more distant ones, and the specified inheritance order is not violated. When I call a method on an object, Python takes the MRO of its class and returns the first implementation found. You can view the order through Cls.mro() or Cls.__mro__.

cards.front: Where does a variable live: on the class or the object, and why it matters
cards.back: A class attribute is declared in the class body outside methods and exists as a single copy - it is shared by all objects. An instance attribute is usually created in __init__ through self.attr and belongs to a specific object. The key point is the lookup order when reading: Python first looks in the object's __dict__, and only if it is empty there - in the class and its MRO. That is why an assignment obj.attr = ... does not change the class attribute but creates a new attribute on the object that "shadows" it.

cards.front: What __enter__ and __exit__ do and how the with block works
cards.back: An object becomes a context manager if it has __enter__ and __exit__. On entering with, Python calls __enter__, and its result is bound to the name after as. On exit - whether normal or due to an exception - Python calls __exit__(exc_type, exc_value, traceback). If there was an exception inside, these three arguments describe it, otherwise they are None. This way resource management does not depend on how we left the block, so a file or connection is guaranteed to be closed.

cards.front: Ports and adapters: how hexagonal architecture isolates the core
cards.back: Alistair Cockburn's idea is to keep the business logic independent of infrastructure details. The core defines ports - the interfaces through which it is used (inbound) and through which it reaches out (outbound). Adapters are implementations of ports for a specific technology: a SQL repository, a REST controller, a console. Dependencies point inward: adapters know about the core, but the core does not know about adapters. This lets you test the logic without a real database (by substituting a fake adapter), and swap storage or interface without changes to the core.

cards.front: Decorator as a way to extend an object's behavior without editing its code
cards.back: The Decorator is a structural pattern that dynamically adds responsibilities to an object without changing its class or spawning subclasses. The object is placed inside a wrapper with the same interface, and the wrapper intercepts calls: it does something of its own before and after, and delegates the actual work to the original object. In Python this is most often implemented through function decorators with the @ syntax, and used for logging, caching, permission checks and similar cross-cutting concerns.

cards.front: Factory Method: how to hand object creation to subclasses
cards.back: The Factory Method moves object creation into a separate method that descendants override. The base class writes its code in terms of the product's common interface and calls factory_method() without knowing which concrete product subclass it will get. As a result, adding a new product type comes down to a new "product + creator" pair without changing existing code - a direct application of the open/closed principle.

cards.front: Why super() exists and how it behaves under multiple inheritance
cards.back: super() returns a proxy that looks for a method not in the current class but further down the MRO chain. This removes the hard dependency on the parent's name - the hierarchy can be rearranged without changing the calls. Under multiple inheritance the key point is that super() goes not "to the parent" but to the next class in the current object's linearized MRO. That's why a chain of cooperative super().__init__() calls visits every ancestor exactly once. The order itself is built by the C3 linearization algorithm.

cards.front: Instance method, classmethod and staticmethod: what's the difference
cards.back: An instance method works with a specific object through self - it reads and changes its attributes. A classmethod works with the class through cls: it changes class attributes or serves as an alternative constructor (from_string, from_dict), and cls correctly picks up the subclass under inheritance. A staticmethod is bound to neither the instance nor the class - it's a helper function that logically lives next to the class but doesn't depend on its state.

cards.front: Metaclass in Python: who creates the class itself, and how
cards.back: A metaclass defines how a class is created. By default all classes are created by the type type, and type is its own metaclass. You write your own metaclass by inheriting from type and overriding __new__ or __init__ to intercept class creation: validate the structure, add methods, register the class somewhere. It should be used rarely: more often the task is solved by class decorators or __init_subclass__. Real-world examples are building models in Django and ORMs, and plugin registration.

cards.front: One interface, many types: polymorphism in the Python standard library
cards.back: Polymorphism gives a single interface for different types. In Python it relies on dynamic typing: what matters is not inheritance but whether the object has the right method. Built-in functions like len(), sorted(), iter() are polymorphic because they delegate the work to dunder methods (__len__, __iter__, comparisons). The dunder __str__ is polymorphic too: each class defines its own string representation. Thanks to this you can write generic functions without tying yourself to the argument's concrete class.

cards.front: Abstract base classes: how to pin down a mandatory interface
cards.back: An ABC defines an interface and guarantees that all descendants fulfill it. In Python this is the abc module: the class inherits from ABC, and mandatory methods are marked with the @abstractmethod decorator. Until a subclass implements all such methods, it cannot be instantiated - the error appears at object creation, not somewhere deep in the middle of execution. ABCs are useful in large projects and team work: you can see what a class must implement, and behavior becomes predictable. An alternative for checking "does it have the required methods" is typing.Protocol and structural typing.

cards.front: Metaclasses: who controls class creation, and why?
cards.back: In Python, classes are objects too, and every object has a type. The type of a class is its metaclass; by default that is type. When the interpreter executes a class statement, it effectively calls the metaclass, passing the name, the bases, and the body namespace, and gets back a finished class. By swapping in a custom metaclass, I hook into this process: I add or validate attributes, register the class, or enforce an invariant across an entire hierarchy. In practice metaclasses are rarely needed - class decorators or __init_subclass__ usually suffice - but in an interview it is important to show that a class is created by a call to its metaclass.

cards.front: classmethod and staticmethod: who gets cls, and who gets nothing
cards.back: Both decorators let you call a method without creating an instance, but they differ in what they receive. A classmethod is bound to the class: through cls it reads and modifies class attributes, and it's handy for alternative constructors (from_string, from_dict). A staticmethod is bound to nothing — it has neither self nor cls, so it fits helper logic that makes sense to keep next to the class. A regular method, by contrast, receives self, that is, a concrete instance.

cards.front: Why pass dependencies in from outside instead of creating them inside the class?
cards.back: DI is an implementation of the Inversion of Control principle: a class doesn't create the objects it needs itself, but receives them ready-made - most often through the constructor, less often via a setter or a method. As a result, the class depends on an abstraction rather than a concrete implementation, so in tests the real dependency is easily swapped for a mock, and in production you can replace the implementation without touching the dependent code. This is a direct consequence of the D in SOLID (Dependency Inversion).

cards.front: What the @dataclass decorator gives you and what boilerplate it removes
cards.back: @dataclass from the dataclasses module (since Python 3.7) automatically generates utility methods based on the declared, type-annotated fields. Instead of hand-writing the constructor, comparison, and string representation, I simply list the fields. Through the decorator's flags you can additionally enable ordering (order=True), immutability (frozen=True), and other methods.

cards.front: __new__ vs __init__: who creates the object, and who configures it
cards.back: When you write MyClass(10), the metaclass runs two steps. First, __new__(cls, ...) is called — this is a static method that creates and returns the instance; its job is to hand back an object. If the returned object is an instance of this class, __init__(self, ...) is then called, which returns nothing (more precisely, it must return None) and only configures state through self. In 99% of code you override only __init__. __new__ is touched rarely: when subclassing immutable types (int, str, tuple), when implementing a singleton or instance caching, and in metaclasses.

cards.front: Iterator vs generator: where is the line?
cards.back: An iterator is defined by a protocol: the object must be able to return itself via __iter__ and yield the next element via __next__, raising StopIteration once exhausted. A generator is a special, most convenient way to obtain an iterator: you write a function with yield, and the interpreter implements the protocol for you, keeping the local state and the suspension point between calls. That is why, for lazy iteration over large or infinite streams, you almost always reach for a generator, and you rarely write __next__ by hand - only when you need non-standard traversal logic.

cards.front: The LEGB rule: how does Python resolve a variable name?
cards.back: When Python encounters a name, it looks for it first in the local scope of the current function, then in enclosing functions (if this one is nested), then among the module's global names, and finally in the built-in namespace, where len, print, Exception live. That is the LEGB rule. Assignment by default creates a local name; to write to a global or an enclosing variable you need global and nonlocal respectively.

cards.front: Anonymous lambda functions: what are they and where do they fit?
cards.back: A lambda is an expression function: it is written as lambda parameters: expression, has no name, and contains no statements, only a single expression to evaluate. Semantically it is no different from an ordinary function created with def - the same function type, just without a bound name. I reach for it where a function is needed on the spot and only once: a sort key, a simple transformation in map, a predicate in filter. If the logic is more than one expression, or the function is reused, I write a regular def.

cards.front: Identity vs equality: when to use is, and when ==
cards.back: is is an identity check: the operator looks at whether two variables refer to the same object (in effect, it compares their id()). == is a value-equality check, and its behavior is defined by the __eq__ method. With None, True, False you always use is, because these are singletons. To compare data - strings, numbers, collections - you use ==.

cards.front: What turns an ordinary object into an iterator?
cards.back: An iterator is an object that implements the iteration protocol of two methods. __iter__() must return the iterator itself, and __next__() yields the next element on each call. When there is nothing more to yield, __next__() raises StopIteration, and the for loop terminates cleanly. It is important not to confuse an iterable (has only __iter__) with an iterator (has both methods): a list is iterable but is not itself an iterator - its __iter__ creates a separate iterator.

cards.front: A class as a decorator: without which method does nothing work?
cards.back: A decorator is any callable object that takes a function and returns a function (or a callable). When a decorator is written as a class, the __init__ constructor remembers the wrapped function, and __call__ runs on each call of the decorated function - that is where the extra logic lives. Without __call__ an instance of the class cannot be called with parentheses, and decoration does not work. The advantage of a class over a function decorator is that it conveniently holds state between calls.

cards.front: Can you apply a decorator manually, without the @ sign?
cards.back: A decorator is an ordinary function that takes a function and returns a new one. Python expands @my_decorator before def func into func = my_decorator(func) right after the definition. So you can do without @: define the function, then call the decorator and assign the result back to the same name. This is useful when you need to apply a decorator conditionally, choose it at runtime, or apply it to a function we did not declare ourselves.

cards.front: Shallow and deep copying of objects: where is the line?
cards.back: The copy module has two functions. A shallow copy (copy) creates a new top-level object, but everything inside is shared by reference: change a nested list in the copy - it changes in the original too. A deep copy (deepcopy) recursively walks the entire structure and creates independent duplicates, so the two branches are no longer connected in any way. I choose based on the task: if detaching the top level is enough - I take copy; if I need full independence with nesting - deepcopy, keeping its time cost in mind.

cards.front: Type dynamics and strictness: which typing model does Python belong to
cards.back: Typing in Python is dynamic and strong. Dynamic, because a type is not declared in advance and is determined at runtime by the value held in a variable; the same name can hold an int today and a str tomorrow. Strong, because the language does not silently add incompatible types together: 1 + "2" raises a TypeError, not "3" or "12". Conversion between types is always explicit. This sets Python apart from weakly typed languages like JavaScript, where such expressions get coerced "somehow".

cards.front: Namespaces and the LEGB rule: how Python finds a variable
cards.back: A namespace is a "name → object" mapping that keeps names from clashing. There are four levels: local (names inside a function), enclosing (names of an outer function as seen by a nested one), global (module level), and built-in (len, print, exceptions). When the interpreter encounters a name, it looks it up by the LEGB rule - first in the local scope, then in the enclosing one, then in the global one, and finally in the built-in scope; the first match wins. This is exactly what determines scope: where a name is accessible and which of several same-named bindings shadows the rest.

cards.front: How to write a decorator that logs the name of the called function
cards.back: A decorator is a callable that receives a function as an argument and returns another function. Inside, I declare wrapper(*args, **kwargs), print func.__name__, and then return func(*args, **kwargs) so the original behavior and its result are preserved. The @decorator syntax above a function definition is just sugar for f = decorator(f). This is how I add logging without touching the function's own code.

cards.front: What are the ways to obtain a generator in Python
cards.back: Most often I write a function with yield - it preserves its state between calls and hands out elements one at a time. For short transformations I use a generator expression (x for x in ...); it's more compact than a list and doesn't materialize the result. If the iteration logic is complex and holds a lot of state, I write an iterator class with __iter__ and __next__ that ends the iteration via raise StopIteration. The main advantage of all the options is laziness: values are computed as they are requested.

cards.front: Decorators in Python: why they are needed and what problems they solve
cards.back: Decorators let you separate auxiliary logic from the main logic. I move recurring concerns - logging, caching, permission checks, timing, input validation - into a separate decorator and attach it to any functions via the @ syntax. Under the hood it's an ordinary higher-order function: it receives the target function as an argument and returns a wrapper that calls the original, surrounding it with extra code. The payoff is reuse, a clean main codebase, and a single point for changes.

cards.front: Why is with integers sometimes lies and sometimes doesn't
cards.back: is answers the question "is this literally the same object?" by comparing id(), while == answers "are the values equal?". CPython pre-creates a pool of small integers from -5 to 256 and reuses them, so a = 100; b = 100 gives a is b == True. But 1000 is 1000 across different variables may turn out False, because these are already different objects. This is a CPython implementation detail, not a language guarantee, so you cannot rely on it. The only reliable scenario for is is checking singletons: is None, is True, is False.

cards.front: How can you view the full set of members of an arbitrary object at runtime?
cards.back: For object introspection I use dir(obj) - it returns a list of strings with all available names, including those inherited from parent classes and base types. If I need only the instance's own attributes, I look at vars(obj) or obj.__dict__; if I need one specific name - getattr and hasattr. dir() with no arguments returns the names from the current local scope.

cards.front: How does the interpreter reclaim unused memory on its own?
cards.back: The base mechanism in CPython is reference counting: every object has a counter, and as soon as it drops to zero, the memory is freed immediately. But reference counting can't handle cycles - two objects that hold references to each other. For that, a cyclic garbage collector from the gc module works on top: it periodically walks container objects, finds unreachable cycles, and frees them. It runs on a generational heuristic, not on every operation.

cards.front: How does filter() help you select elements by a condition?
cards.back: filter() takes a predicate function and an iterable, runs the predicate over each element, and returns an iterator of the elements where the result was truthy. It's a lazy object: elements are computed on demand, so I wrap it in list() or tuple() when I need a materialized result. If you pass None instead of a function, filter() drops every element that is falsy by its boolean value.

cards.front: What is a hash, and where in Python can't you do without it?
cards.back: A hash function maps an input of arbitrary length to a value of fixed size. In Python the built-in hash() produces an integer for immutable objects - it is exactly this value that lets dict and set find an element in almost constant time. For robust cryptographic hashes (SHA-256 and the like) there is the hashlib module. The key property: the same input always gives the same hash, and equal objects must have equal hashes - otherwise the dictionary breaks.

cards.front: What does a dictionary's items() method return, and how do you use it?
cards.back: items() gives a dict_items - a dynamic view of the dictionary's pairs, not a copy of them. You iterate over it, unpacking each element into key, value. The view is live: if the dictionary changes, so does what is visible through items(). When needed it can be materialized with list(), but for simple iteration that is unnecessary.

cards.front: Which collections can you build with a comprehension?
cards.back: Comprehensions exist for lists [...], sets {...}, dictionaries {k: v ...}, and generators (...). They share the same skeleton: an expression, a for loop, and an optional if filter. The list, set, and dict forms build the whole collection at once, while the generator form returns a lazy object that computes elements on demand and saves memory. There is no tuple comprehension: (...) is a generator expression, not a tuple.

cards.front: How do you get an element's position right inside a loop over a sequence?
cards.back: The idiomatic way is for i, value in enumerate(seq). This is better than for i in range(len(seq)) with a seq[i] access, because the code reads cleaner and does not depend on whether the object supports indexing. enumerate has a second argument, start, for when numbering should begin somewhere other than zero.

cards.front: What is a closure, and how does a function remember outer variables?
cards.back: A closure arises when an inner function references a variable from the enclosing scope and that function "lives" longer than its creator. The captured variables are stored in cells, accessible via __closure__. Function factories and decorators are built on closures. If inside the closure you need to assign to an outer variable rather than just read it, you need nonlocal, otherwise Python creates a new local variable.

cards.front: What built-in data types does Python have, and how can you group them
cards.back: Python has numeric types (int, float, complex), the boolean bool, the string str, collections (list, tuple, set, dict), and a separate NoneType with the single value None. Collections split into mutable (list, set, dict) and immutable (tuple, str). bool is a subclass of int, so True equals 1 and False equals 0.

cards.front: Why does a class need __slots__, and at what cost does it save memory
cards.back: By default each instance stores its attributes in a __dict__ dictionary, which is flexible but memory-hungry. If you declare __slots__ with a list of names, Python allocates compact slots for those attributes instead of a dictionary. This gives a noticeable memory saving with millions of objects and slightly speeds up reads/writes. The price is a loss of dynamism: you can no longer add new, undeclared attributes.

cards.front: Decorators: adding behavior to a function without touching its code
cards.back: A decorator relies on the fact that functions in Python are first-class objects: they can be passed around and returned. The decorator takes a function as an argument, builds a wrapper inside that does something before and after calling the original, and returns that wrapper instead of the original function. The @timing notation is exactly equivalent to func = timing(func). This is how cross-cutting logic - logging, timing, caching, permission checks - is factored into one place and applied to any function without changing its body.

cards.front: Generic types: one piece of code for any type, checked by the type checker
cards.back: A generic lets you write a structure or function once so that it works with any type, while the type checker (mypy, pyright) sees the concrete type at every use site. You declare the type parameter with TypeVar('T') and inherit the class from Generic[T]. When you write Stack[int], the checker knows pop() returns an int and catches an error before runtime. Important: in Python itself these are annotations, not runtime generics - at runtime Stack[int] and Stack[str] behave identically, there is no type checking during execution, everything happens at the code-analysis stage.

cards.front: Why plain datetime is not enough for time zones and why pytz exists
cards.back: On its own datetime creates "naive" objects with no zone attached: it computes intervals, formats and parses, but it does not care about the UTC offset or DST. To get an "aware" object you need a tzinfo implementation. Historically this came from pytz, because it ships the full IANA database and handles DST transitions correctly. In Python 3.9+ the built-in zoneinfo was added for this purpose, which reads the same database and is preferable in new projects.

cards.front: isinstance vs type: which type-check to choose and why
cards.back: type(x) is int is true only when x is exactly int; for any descendant the check fails. isinstance(x, int) returns True both for int and for its subclasses — this aligns with the Liskov substitution principle, which is why code almost always picks it. Bonus: isinstance accepts a tuple of types — isinstance(x, (int, float)). The case where you deliberately reach for type() is when you need to reject subclasses and check for an exact class match.

cards.front: How Pydantic validates data against annotations and what exactly it guarantees
cards.back: Pydantic describes a schema through classes that subclass BaseModel with annotated fields. At initialization time it runs each field through validation against its type and any extra constraints (gt, max_length, etc.). If a value doesn't fit, a ValidationError is raised that lists all the fields that failed and the reasons, not just the first error. An important detail: by default Pydantic coerces compatible values (the string "30" into int), and strict behavior is enabled via Strict types or model_config.

cards.front: range is an iterable, not an iterator: what's the difference and why it matters
cards.back: There are two distinct concepts. An iterable can hand out an iterator on demand via __iter__ — these include lists, strings, range. An iterator yields elements one at a time via __next__ and is single-use: once it reaches the end it is exhausted. range belongs to the first category: every call to iter(range(5)) creates a new, independent iterator, while the range object itself stays unchanged and reusable. On top of that it stores only the bounds and the step, so it takes constant memory regardless of length.

cards.front: How to build a list in one line with a list comprehension?
cards.back: A list comprehension lets you describe building a list declaratively: [expression for item in source if condition]. It reads left to right as "take each element of the source, filter it if needed, and put the applied expression into the result." Compared with a manual loop and append, this form is shorter, more expressive and usually a bit faster, because the loop runs at the interpreter level rather than in Python code with a method call on every iteration.

cards.front: How Python frees memory on its own: the design of the garbage collector
cards.back: CPython has two levels of memory management. The first and main one is reference counting: every object has a reference count, and when it reaches zero the object is freed immediately. This mechanism is deterministic and fast, but it has a blind spot — circular references: two objects reference each other, their counts never drop to zero, even though they are unreachable from the outside. So there's a second level — the cyclic collector from the gc module, which periodically walks container objects, finds unreachable cycles and frees them. It can be controlled manually: run it, disable it, change the thresholds.

cards.front: How do you re-raise a caught exception without swallowing it?
cards.back: If I caught an exception but can't or shouldn't handle it fully, I run a local reaction (for example, logging) and then write raise with no arguments. That doesn't create a new exception; it propagates the same one up the stack with its traceback untouched. I won't silence the error with an empty except or by returning None - that just hides the problem.

cards.front: Why does deep recursion in Python hit a ceiling and slow down?
cards.back: Python has no tail-call optimization, and every recursive call pushes a new frame onto the stack. To avoid an overflow, the interpreter enforces a depth limit (~1000) and raises RecursionError when it's exceeded. You can raise the limit via sys.setrecursionlimit, but that only moves the ceiling and risks crashing the process against the real C stack. For deep problems, I rewrite recursion into an iterative form with an explicit stack.

cards.front: How do you swap two variables in a single line?
cards.back: In Python the swap is one line: a, b = b, a. No intermediate variable is needed, because the right side first evaluates the whole tuple (b, a) from the current values, and only then unpacks its elements into a and b. It's idiomatic, readable, and works for any number of variables.

cards.front: itertools: what iterator building blocks does the standard library give you
cards.back: itertools splits its tools into three groups. Infinite generators: count, cycle, repeat. Functions that shorten or filter an input stream: chain, compress, dropwhile, takewhile, groupby, islice. And combinatorics: product, permutations, combinations. The key idea is that they all return a lazy iterator rather than a list, so you can assemble them into a pipeline and apply it to data that doesn't fit in memory all at once.

cards.front: A function as an argument: what does it mean for functions to be "first-class" in Python
cards.back: Functions in Python are first-class objects. You can assign them to variables, put them in lists and dicts, return them from other functions, and pass them as arguments. This is exactly what map, filter, sorted(key=...), decorators, and callbacks rely on. By passing behavior as a parameter, you lift the varying part out and reuse a common skeleton instead of copying code.

cards.front: A mutable object as a default value: why it's a classic trap
cards.back: Default arguments are created once, at the moment def is compiled, and stored in func.__defaults__. When the default is a mutable object, all calls without an explicit argument share the same object, and edits to it survive the call. The standard fix: use None as the default and create the real object inside the function body when arg is None. That way each call gets a fresh copy.

cards.front: What is match/case and why isn't it just a switch?
cards.back: match/case appeared in 3.10 and implements pattern matching, not a simple choice by equality. Beyond comparing against constants, it can unpack sequences and dicts, check classes, and bind matched parts to free names right inside the pattern. The case _ pattern catches everything else, like default. The main difference from a switch: I'm matching a value not against a value, but against the shape of the data.

cards.front: Can you change a single character of a string in place?
cards.back: A string is an immutable object, so you can't assign s[1] = 'a' - it raises a TypeError. To get a modified version, you build a new string with slices and concatenation, or temporarily switch to a list of characters. Immutability gives strings hashability, safety when passed around, and the ability to be interned.

cards.front: How do you check whether an object can be used as a dictionary key?
cards.back: Hashability is the property of an object being reducible to an integer hash that stays constant for the object's lifetime. Immutable types are usually hashable: numbers, strings, tuples of hashable elements, frozenset. Mutable containers - list, dict, set - are not hashable, because their hash could change. You can check by calling hash() inside a try/except TypeError, or via isinstance(obj, collections.abc.Hashable).

cards.front: How do you materialize a generator into a regular list?
cards.back: A generator is a lazy iterator, so on its own it is not a list. To get a list, I pass the generator to list(): this function iterates over it until StopIteration and puts the elements into a new list. After that you get index access, slices, and all list methods. There is one downside: the whole sequence ends up in memory at once, so for infinite or very large generators you should not do this.

cards.front: What is an iterator, and which iterators do you encounter in Python?
cards.back: An iterator is an object that has __next__(), which returns the next element, and __iter__(), which returns the iterator itself. When the elements run out, __next__() raises StopIteration, and the for loop catches it. A list on its own is iterable but not an iterator; iter() turns it into one. In the standard library, iterators include generators (functions with yield), as well as the lazy results of map(), filter(), zip(), enumerate, and objects from itertools.

cards.front: Can you apply a decorator to a class rather than a function?
cards.back: A decorator is any callable that takes another object and returns a replacement. For a class, the syntax @deco above class C is equivalent to C = deco(C). Inside the decorator I can add methods or attributes to the class, wrap __init__, or replace the class with another. This is handy for cross-cutting functionality - logging, registration in a registry, caching, permission checks - without editing the class body. If you need to intervene deeper, in the creation of the class itself, you use metaclasses, but for most tasks a decorator is enough.

cards.front: Why can't you access a generator by index?
cards.back: Indexing requires the __getitem__ protocol, which a generator does not have - it implements only the iterator protocol (__next__). A generator computes values lazily and does not keep past ones in memory, so the notion of "element number N" simply does not exist for it without iteration. If I need a specific element by position, I either iterate the generator myself, or use itertools.islice, or, if there is little data, materialize it into a list with list() and then work with the list.

cards.front: Iterating a dictionary by pairs: why you need the items() method
cards.back: For simultaneous access to a key and a value I use dict.items(). The method returns not a list but a view object - a dynamic representation that reflects the dictionary's current state. In the loop I unpack each pair into two variables: for key, value in d.items():. If I need only the keys, for k in d (or .keys()) is enough; if only the values, .values().

cards.front: What exactly does a decorator return, and what replaces the original function
cards.back: A decorator is a callable that takes a function and returns a function. Writing @deco above def f is syntactic sugar for f = deco(f). So the decorator's return value is exactly what ends up under the name f. Usually you return an inner wrapper function that calls the original and adds logic before and after. So that the wrapper does not "eat" the arguments and the result, it is written as def wrapper(*args, **kwargs): return func(*args, **kwargs), and the original's metadata is preserved via functools.wraps.

cards.front: Catch an exception, react to it, and re-raise it up the stack
