cards.back: When I need to do something at the point of an error but not swallow it, I use try/except and inside the except I write a bare raise - it re-raises the current exception, preserving the original traceback. This is not the same as raise e: a bare raise leaves the trace as is, whereas raise e or raise NewError() may overwrite the original point of failure. If I need to substitute the error type but not lose the cause, I use raise NewError(...) from e - this links the exceptions into a chain.

cards.front: Python exception hierarchy: built-in classes and your own subclasses
cards.back: Exceptions in Python are classes arranged in a hierarchy. At the top is BaseException; from it branch the system-level ones (SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt) and the general Exception, from which almost everything application-level inherits. Under Exception there are meaningful groups: ArithmeticError (with ZeroDivisionError, OverflowError), LookupError (with IndexError, KeyError), as well as ValueError, TypeError, OSError, ImportError and others. I make my own exceptions subclasses of Exception (or a narrower class) so they can be caught both individually and through the base type.

cards.front: Which Python objects can serve as a decorator?
cards.back: A decorator is simply a callable that receives a function (or class) and returns something to replace it. Most often it is a function returning a closure wrapper, but classes work just the same (their instance replaces the original function), as do objects with a __call__ method. The syntax @dec above def f is sugar for f = dec(f). It is used to attach cross-cutting logic — logging, caching, permission checks — without rewriting the function itself.

cards.front: What is the difference between @dec and @dec() when applying a decorator?
cards.back: It all comes down to one level of calling. With @dec the interpreter does f = dec(f) — so dec must accept the function itself. With @dec(arg), dec(arg) runs first, giving f = dec(arg)(f) — meaning dec here is not a decorator but a decorator factory: it takes parameters and returns the actual decorator. That is why a parameterized decorator has three nested levels: the factory, the decorator, and the wrapper.

cards.front: How does type conversion work in Python: explicit versus implicit?
cards.back: Python has two forms. Explicit — you call the type constructor yourself: int("42"), float(7), str(5) — and get a new object of the desired type. Implicit — the interpreter widens the type on its own in mixed arithmetic so as not to lose precision: 5 + 2.5 yields a float. Important: Python does not perform implicit conversions between incompatible types — you cannot add a number and a string, you get a TypeError. So implicitness is mostly limited to the numeric tower (int -> float -> complex).

cards.front: Swapping code on the fly: why monkey patching matters in tests
cards.back: Monkey patching is the dynamic replacement of an attribute, function, or method at runtime. In tests I use it to isolate a module from the network, the database, or third-party libraries: I substitute a stub that returns a known answer in advance. This makes tests fast, repeatable, and independent of external services. In practice I usually do it through unittest.mock.patch, so the substitution is rolled back automatically after the test.

cards.front: unittest, pytest, and mock: how these tools differ in practice
cards.back: My baseline set is this: pytest as the main runner, because it is more concise and extends through plugins, and unittest.mock for isolation from external systems. I know and use unittest where I don't want to pull in dependencies: it ships in the standard library and is built on classes that subclass TestCase. The main difference with pytest is a plain assert instead of a set of assertEqual methods, plus powerful fixtures and parametrization. And mock handles a different job: it doesn't run tests, it creates fake objects so you can check logic in isolation from the database, the network, and third-party APIs.

cards.front: What a mock object is and why you need it in testing
cards.back: A mock is a stub object that replaces a real dependency in a test. I give it the return values or exceptions I need and substitute it for the real service. This gives you four things: isolation of the code under test, speed (no waiting on the network and the database), full control over scenarios, and independence from the state of external systems. As a bonus, a mock remembers how it was called — you can verify that the code reached the dependency with the right arguments.

cards.front: Levels of testing: from unit checks to acceptance
cards.back: I distinguish four levels. Unit tests check an isolated piece of code — a function or a class — and are written by the developers themselves. Integration tests look at how modules fit together. System tests run the whole product under conditions close to production. Acceptance tests confirm that the system covers the user's requirements. In practice this is a pyramid: many fast, cheap unit tests at the base and a few expensive end-to-end checks at the top. Acceptance System / integration Unit

cards.front: Integration tests vs. functional tests: what exactly does each one check?
cards.back: These are two cross-sections with different points of view. An integration test takes several modules already verified separately, connects them, and checks that data isn't lost or distorted at the boundaries — that is, it tests the interaction. A functional test works like a black box: we feed an input, compare the output against the requirement, and how exactly it was computed inside doesn't matter. One answers the question "do the modules get along?", the other "does the product do what was promised to the user?".

cards.front: Parametrizing tests: how to run one test against a dozen data sets?
cards.back: When you need to check a function across many cases, the bad path is to breed near-identical tests that differ only in numbers. Parametrization solves this with a single decorator: I specify the parameter names and a list of value sets, and pytest expands them into separate test cases by itself. Each set runs in isolation, and the report shows exactly which case failed instead of "the whole test is red." This gives more coverage, less duplication, and a negligible cost for adding a new case — append a tuple and you're done.

cards.front: Why do teams choose pytest over the standard unittest?
cards.back: The main argument is less ceremony for the same or greater expressiveness. In unittest a test must live as a method inside a class that subclasses TestCase, and assertions are made with methods like assertEqual. In pytest a plain function and a simple assert are enough, and on failure pytest parses the expression itself and shows which values didn't match. On top of that: automatic test discovery, concise parametrization, powerful fixtures with scopes, and a large ecosystem of plugins (coverage, Django, CI). At the same time pytest can also run existing unittest tests, so the migration doesn't have to be abrupt.

cards.front: Fixtures in pytest: how to prepare the test environment without duplication?
cards.back: Fixtures solve the problem of repeated setup and cleanup. For example, tests need a clean database before each run — instead of duplicating setup and teardown in every test, I move it into a function with the @pytest.fixture decorator. The test declares the fixture name as an argument, pytest calls it itself and substitutes the result. Through yield a fixture can also release resources after the test. On top of that: control over how often it is recreated via scope, parametrization, and automatic attachment via autouse. The result: cleaner tests, no copy-paste, and data preparation living in one place.

cards.front: Testing coroutines: organizing tests for async functions
cards.back: You test an async function the same way as a regular one, but the test framework needs an event loop. In practice I use pytest with the pytest-asyncio plugin: I mark the test @pytest.mark.asyncio and write await right in the body. If you want to stick to the standard library, there's unittest.IsolatedAsyncioTestCase - it gives each test an isolated loop. The main thing is not to forget to await the coroutine: a bare call to an async function returns a coroutine object, not a result, and asserting on it checks nothing.

cards.front: Fixture lifetime: how scope in pytest controls resource reuse
cards.back: A fixture has four main lifetime levels: function - recreated for each test (the default), class - one instance per test class, module - one per file, session - one for the whole run. A narrow scope gives maximum isolation but pays in time on repeated setup/teardown. A wide scope speeds up the run by reusing an expensive resource like a DB or a network connection, but it requires that tests don't corrupt the shared state. Choosing a scope is a trade-off between isolation and speed.

cards.front: Substituting dependencies in tests: what patch from unittest.mock is for
cards.back: patch temporarily replaces an attribute in a given place with a mock and automatically rolls the substitution back when the test finishes. I use it when the code reaches into the outside world - API, DB, files, time - and I want to test the logic, not a third-party service. The key point: you patch where the name is used, not where it's defined. If a function does import requests; requests.get(...), I patch requests.get, but if the module says from requests import get, I have to patch my_module.get. You can apply patch as a decorator, as a context manager, or manually via start()/stop().

cards.front: Software life cycle: what phases a product goes through from idea to maintenance?
cards.back: The SDLC is a standardized scheme for a software product's life, broken into phases: planning, requirements, design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase has its own input, output, and area of responsibility. I lean on this model so the team understands which stage we're at, which artifact we should produce, and who owns it. How the phases are actually carried out depends on the methodology: in waterfall they run strictly one after another; in Agile they're cycled through on every iteration.

cards.front: How do waterfall, Agile, Scrum, and Kanban differ and when do you pick each?
cards.back: I split the approaches into two groups. Waterfall is linear: a phase finishes completely before the next one begins; it works well where requirements are fixed and stable. Agile is a family of iterative approaches: we work in short cycles, each ending with a working increment, and requirements are refined along the way. Scrum is a specific framework within Agile with roles, sprints, and ceremonies; Kanban is a continuous-flow method with a visualized board and a limit on work in progress (WIP). The choice depends on the stability of requirements and the nature of the flow: a fixed scope - waterfall, a changing product with sprint-based releases - Scrum, a flow of heterogeneous tasks without clear iterations - Kanban.

cards.front: What steps actually make up the path from a product idea to its maintenance?
cards.back: I break it into six steps. First, requirements analysis - we figure out whose problems we're solving and which ones, what the business goals and constraints are. Next, design - we turn the requirements into architecture, schemas, and prototypes. Then development - we write code and wire components together. Testing verifies conformance to requirements and the absence of defects. Deployment brings the product into operation. And maintenance keeps it alive: bugs, new features, performance. The key idea is that each step relies on the result of the previous one, so a mistake during analysis costs the most by the end.

cards.front: TDD: what does writing the test before the code mean, and where are its limits?
cards.back: TDD is development through the "red - green - refactor" cycle. First I write a test for behavior that doesn't exist yet, and it fails (red). Then I add the minimum code to make the test pass (green). Then I clean up the code without changing behavior, leaning on the test as a safety net (refactor). The upsides: code is thought through for edge cases in advance, regressions are caught instantly, refactoring is safe, and the tests serve as living documentation. The downsides: it's slower at the start, it demands the discipline to stick to the cycle, and it doesn't fit hard-to-test layers like UI. I apply TDD where the logic is clear and lends itself to unit tests, and I don't force it onto parts that are hard to isolate.

cards.front: What's behind the KISS principle and how does it shape code?
cards.back: KISS is the stance of not over-complicating without need. I phrase it like this: a solution should be exactly as complex as the task requires, and not a gram more. In practice that means choosing a straightforward algorithm over a clever one, a clear name over an explanatory comment, a flat structure over extra layers of abstraction. The payoff is in the cost of ownership: a new person gets into the project faster, bugs are easier to track down, changes go in without fear of breaking something. It's important not to confuse KISS with primitiveness: the goal isn't "less code at any cost" but minimal complexity while preserving correctness and readability.

cards.front: What is the point of the DRY rule, and where does it start to hurt?
cards.back: DRY is about removing duplication of knowledge, not just of similar lines. If the same business logic or formula lives in several places, every change forces you to edit each copy, and it's easy to miss one - which leads to drift and bugs. So I move repeated logic into a single function, class, or module with one source of truth. But there's an important caveat: DRY is about knowledge, not about superficial code similarity. Two chunks that look identical today but change for different reasons should not be merged - that's false DRY, which you later have to painfully untangle. A sound guideline is the rule of three: I tolerate the first repetition and extract an abstraction at the third.

cards.front: Agile: what idea stands behind agile development?
cards.back: Agile is the philosophy of flexible development described in the Agile Manifesto. Its essence: value individuals and interactions over processes and tools, a working product over documentation, collaboration with the customer over a formal contract, and responding to change over following a plan. In practice this is embodied by concrete approaches - Scrum, Kanban, Lean. Work is broken into short iterations (sprints) of 1-4 weeks; after each one the team shows results and corrects course. The main benefit is fast adaptation to changing requirements and the market.

cards.front: How Scrum is structured: roles, artifacts, events
cards.back: Scrum is a framework on top of Agile values with iterative-incremental development. It rests on three pillars. Roles: the Product Owner is responsible for value and priorities, the Scrum Master for the process and removing impediments, and the development team builds the product. Artifacts: the Product Backlog (the full list of requirements), the Sprint Backlog (the tasks for the current sprint), and the Increment (the finished result of the sprint). Events: the Sprint itself (1-4 weeks), Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and Sprint Retrospective. The key idea is a short, fixed cycle at the end of which there is a potentially shippable product and feedback.

cards.front: CI, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment: where is the line?
cards.back: CI (Continuous Integration) is the frequent merging of changes into a shared repository, where every commit triggers an automatic build and tests; the goal is to catch conflicts and breakages early. CD expands two ways. Continuous Delivery means changes are automatically prepared and promoted all the way up to the production environment, but the final push to production is triggered by a human pressing a button. Continuous Deployment goes further: everything that passes the tests deploys itself to production with no manual step. So the line between Delivery and Deployment is the presence of a manual approval before the live environment. CI is about quality; CD is about the speed and reliability of delivery.

cards.front: How to choose between Django and FastAPI for a specific task
cards.back: Put it like this: Django is a "batteries included" framework. The core already ships an ORM, a migration system, forms, templates, an auto-generated admin, and a user subsystem with permissions. That pays off on monoliths with rich domain logic and server-side HTML delivery - features come together quickly because almost everything is already written for you. FastAPI is a microframework. It is async by default, validates and serializes incoming data from type annotations via Pydantic, and publishes an OpenAPI schema on its own. Its territory is fast JSON APIs, microservices, and gateways. The bottom line in one phrase: you pick Django for completeness and development pace on a complex product, and FastAPI for lightness, strict typing, and native async for APIs.

cards.front: Monolith vs. microservices: when splitting helps and when it hurts
cards.back: A monolith keeps the interface, business logic, and data access in one deployable block: it is easy to launch and debug, and internal calls are cheap (everything runs in one process). The price is coupling - a single part cannot be scaled or released independently, and as it grows the codebase gets heavier. Microservices cut the system into autonomous services that talk over the network through APIs: they scale and release separately, and teams work in parallel. But the cost of being distributed appears - network latency, orchestration, monitoring, and harder debugging. So for a small project or a small team I start with a monolith and move to microservices when coupling and release frequency genuinely become the bottleneck.

cards.front: A message's journey inside RabbitMQ: from producer to consumer
cards.back: The key idea: a producer publishes not to a queue but to an exchange. The exchange looks at its type and its bindings and decides which queues to send a copy of the message to. A binding links an exchange to a queue by routing key or headers. After that the message sits in the queue until a consumer pulls it out. So the full path is: producer → exchange → binding → queue → consumer. If no binding matches, the message is either dropped or routed to an alternate exchange. Producer Exchange Binding routing Queue Consumer

cards.front: Why does a message leave the queue only after processing is acknowledged?
cards.back: Deletion-on-acknowledgment exists for at-least-once semantics. Reading and deletion are separated: the consumer first takes the message, processes it, and only then confirms success. If it crashes before confirming, the message becomes available again and another consumer picks it up. In SQS a message that has been read is hidden for the duration of the Visibility Timeout, and if no DeleteMessage arrives within that window, it returns to the queue. In RabbitMQ, with manual ack, the message stays held by the consumer until confirmation arrives; if the connection drops without an ack, the broker returns it to the queue. The price of reliability is the possibility of redelivery, so the handler must be idempotent.

cards.front: Message delivery semantics in Kafka: from at-most-once to exactly-once
cards.back: Delivery semantics are determined by the combination of producer settings and consumer behavior. At-most-once: the producer waits for no acknowledgments (acks=0), a message may be lost but it's fast - fine for metrics where losing some data isn't critical. At-least-once: the producer waits for an acknowledgment and retries on timeout, so duplicates are possible - this is the default for most tasks, and the receiver must be idempotent. Exactly-once: producer idempotence and transactions are added, duplicates are eliminated - this is for finance and similar critical scenarios, but at the cost of performance. The key thought: "exactly once" in Kafka is not magic, it's an idempotent producer plus a transactional write, not just a checkbox.

cards.front: Producer and consumer: two roles in asynchronous data exchange
cards.back: Producer and consumer are roles, not necessarily separate services: one component can be both, in different threads. A producer puts a message into a topic or queue and does not know who will read it or when. A consumer is subscribed to a topic and picks up messages, processing them at its own pace. The point of this decoupling is that the sides do not block each other: the producer doesn't wait for processing, and the consumer doesn't wait synchronously for data to appear. The broker between them acts as a buffer, which provides scalability, fault tolerance, and load-peak smoothing.

cards.front: Kafka vs. RabbitMQ: when to reach for an event log and when for a queue broker
cards.back: Frame the difference through the data model, not through a feature list: **Kafka stores a log.** A message is not deleted after being read - it is read by offset, and retention is defined by time or size. Several consumer groups read one topic independently. This gives replay, analytics, and high throughput. **RabbitMQ delivers to a queue.** A message lives until it is taken and acknowledged (ack), after which it disappears. The route is set by the exchange (direct, fanout, topic, headers) and queue bindings. **Scaling.** In Kafka parallelism is bounded by the number of topic partitions. In RabbitMQ - by the number of consumers on a queue and the sharding scheme. **When to use which.** Kafka - streaming of logs, metrics, event sourcing, real-time analytics. RabbitMQ - task queues, RPC, integrating systems with fine-grained routing and priorities. A short takeaway for the interviewer: "Kafka is a replayable log built for high load, RabbitMQ is a broker with rich routing and per-message delivery."

cards.front: Name the common architectural styles and explain the problem each one solves
cards.back: "I split them into two groups. The first is patterns for organizing the UI layer: MVC, MVP, and MVVM. They differ in who acts as the mediator between data and screen - a controller, a presenter, or a viewmodel with binding. The second group is system styles: client-server as the baseline, microservices for independent deployment and scaling, P2P for distributing load without a central node, and event-driven architecture for asynchronous reaction to changes. The choice of style is dictated not by fashion but by which property of the system you need to strengthen: testability, independence of parts, or fault tolerance."

cards.front: MVC vs MVVM: where the line runs and who binds data to the screen
cards.back: "MVC and MVVM both split an application into three layers, and the Model is the same in both - data and business logic. The difference is who connects the data to the screen. In MVC that's the controller: it takes input, mutates the model, and decides what to show, and the View often pulls data straight from the model. In MVVM a ViewModel appears - it prepares data for display and exposes observable properties, and the View is bound to them. So when the data changes the interface updates itself, with no explicit code. MVC is simpler and a good fit for the web; MVVM wins in rich desktop and mobile interfaces with lots of two-way interaction."

cards.front: How microservices talk to each other: synchronous calls vs queues
cards.back: "I start picking a communication style with one question: do I need a response right now? If yes - it's a synchronous call. REST over HTTP fits almost everywhere: simple, readable, debuggable from the browser, but verbose and slower. gRPC over HTTP/2 with Protocol Buffers gives low latency and compact messages, with typed contracts from a .proto file - I reach for it for internal high-load calls. If I don't need a response right now, or decoupling the services matters, I take a message queue: the producer drops a message, the consumer picks it up when it's ready. This buys peak buffering, retries, and fault tolerance, but we pay for it with complexity and with the result becoming eventual rather than instant."

cards.front: API Gateway: why a system needs a single entry point in front of microservices
cards.back: "An API Gateway is a single entry point in front of a swarm of microservices. Instead of the client knowing the addresses of dozens of services and assembling the data itself, it sends one request to the gateway. The gateway decides which service to route the request to, checks authentication and authorization at the door, aggregates responses from several services into one when needed, caches frequent responses, and writes metrics and logs. This simplifies the client, hides the internal topology, and moves cross-cutting logic - security, throttling, monitoring - into one place. The downside is that the gateway becomes a single point of failure and a bottleneck, so it's kept fault-tolerant and isn't weighed down with business logic."

cards.front: Vertical vs horizontal scaling: how they differ and when to choose which?
cards.back: There are two basic approaches. Vertical scaling means growing the resources of a single machine: we add RAM, CPU, disk. The upside - you don't have to touch the application's architecture; the downside - there's a physical ceiling and usually downtime during the upgrade. Horizontal scaling means adding new nodes and distributing the load across them through a balancer. The upside - it scales almost linearly and survives the failure of individual nodes; the downside - the application must be ready for distributed operation: stateless services, shared state storage, data consistency. In practice monoliths gravitate toward vertical, while distributed systems and microservices lean horizontal, and the two are often combined.

cards.front: The CAP theorem: why a distributed system can't have it all at once?
cards.back: The CAP theorem (Brewer's theorem, 2000) says a distributed system can't simultaneously provide consistency, availability, and partition tolerance - at most two of the three. The subtlety is that in a distributed system a network partition happens on its own, so P can't be dropped. That means the real choice is between CP and AP. A CP system, during a network split, prefers to stop and not serve potentially stale data (this is what banking systems do, for example). An AP system keeps answering, but the data on the nodes temporarily diverges (this is how social networks and feeds work). The CA option exists only in theory - in a system without network partitions, which is effectively a single node.

cards.front: Event-driven architecture (EDA): how components communicate through events?
cards.back: EDA is an architecture in which interaction is built around events rather than direct calls. An event is a message about a significant state change: "order placed", "payment went through". There are three roles: producers (publishers) generate events, consumers are subscribed to them and react, and a broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SNS) receives events from producers and delivers them to consumers. The main advantage is loose coupling: the producer doesn't know who or how many consumers will process the event, so new consumers can be added without changing existing code. We pay for this with harder debugging, the need to make consumers idempotent, and consistency becoming eventual.

cards.front: Microservice resilience patterns: Circuit Breaker, Retry, Fallback, and company
cards.back: I'll name five core patterns. **Timeout** caps how long you wait for a response, otherwise a stuck call holds resources. **Retry** automatically repeats a request on a transient failure, ideally with exponential backoff and jitter. **Circuit Breaker** tracks errors and, when there are too many, "trips open": for some time calls fail immediately without reaching the sick service, then it tries to half-close and check for recovery. **Fallback** gives a degraded response on failure - a cache or a stub. **Bulkhead** isolates resources (for example, separate thread pools) so that overload of one integrated service doesn't eat the resources for everyone else. In practice they're combined: Timeout + Retry + Circuit Breaker + Fallback is a typical bundle for an outgoing call.

cards.front: Why is a Kafka topic split into partitions and what does it give the system?
cards.back: A topic in Kafka is a logical stream of events, and a partition is its physical unit of parallelism. Each partition is an ordered, append-only log that lives entirely on one broker and is replicated to others. By splitting a topic into N partitions, we get N independent streams that can be spread across different brokers and read by N consumers simultaneously. The price for this is ordering: it's preserved within a partition, but there's no global ordering across partitions. That's why the message key is chosen so that events whose sequence matters land in the same partition.

cards.front: Load balancer: what problem does it solve and which algorithms spread traffic?
cards.back: A load balancer solves three problems at once: availability, scalability, and performance. A single entry point sits in front of the server pool, distributes requests across the backends, and removes failed nodes from rotation via health checks. Thanks to this, the pool scales horizontally by adding nodes, and the failure of one server is invisible to the user. You pick the algorithm based on the load profile: round-robin for uniform short requests, least connections for requests of varying duration, and a hash by key when you need to pin a client to a specific server (sticky sessions).

cards.front: Direct vs topic exchange in RabbitMQ: what is the fundamental routing difference?
cards.back: In RabbitMQ a producer does not send a message to a queue directly but to an exchange, and the exchange type determines the routing rule. Direct is an exact string match: the routing key must literally equal the queue's binding key, otherwise the message won't land there. Topic is a pattern match over words separated by dots: * stands for one word, # for any number of them. You use direct for simple routing by fixed categories, and topic when the key encodes several dimensions (for example region.service.level) and subscribers want to select slices by any of them.

cards.front: What does the CAP theorem actually claim, and how should you read it?
cards.back: I'd stress an important subtlety that people often fail on: "pick 2 of 3" is a simplification. Partition tolerance (P) is not optional for a distributed system: the network always breaks, and the system is simply obligated to survive it. So the real choice is between C and A, and it arises precisely at the moment of a partition. If nodes lose connectivity, you can either refuse to answer for the sake of consistency (CP) or keep answering, risking serving stale data (AP). Examples of CP are HBase and MongoDB in strict mode; examples of AP are DynamoDB and Cassandra. "CA systems" in the strict sense are about the non-distributed scenario; for a distributed database that option doesn't work.

cards.front: When to choose a monolith, and when to split the system into services?
cards.back: I look at four things: the size and complexity of the domain, the team structure, the load profile, and the maturity of the infrastructure. If the application is compact, there is a single team, and the load is even, I start with a monolith: it's cheaper to build, test, and ship, and transactions stay within a single process. I reach for microservices when parts of the system need to be scaled and released independently, different teams work on them, and we already have orchestration, monitoring, and tracing. Microservices are not "better" - they are a trade of simplicity for flexibility: you introduce a network between components, distributed transactions, and operational overhead. That's why by default I start with a monolith and carve out services where the pain becomes concrete.

cards.front: Client and server: who calls whom, and why split the roles
cards.back: The client-server model splits responsibilities between two sides. The client is the initiator: it forms a request and waits for a result (for example, a browser opening a page). The server passively listens, accepts incoming requests, does the work, and returns a response. The main benefit of this approach is that the data and access rules are centralized on the server: they are easier to update, secure, and scale under load, because a single server serves many clients at once.

cards.front: REST, GraphQL, gRPC, and SOAP: how the API styles differ in practice
cards.back: REST organizes the API around resources and standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE); it is stateless and cacheable, which makes it the default for most public APIs thanks to its simplicity. GraphQL lets the client request exactly the fields it needs through a single entry point, eliminating over-fetching and under-fetching when different clients need different slices of data. gRPC is built on top of HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers: the binary format and multiplexing give high performance and streaming, which is why it is chosen for internal communication between microservices. SOAP is an old XML protocol with a strict specification and WS-Security; it is kept around in enterprise and banking systems where a rigid contract and built-in security matter.

cards.front: How do you organize calls to third-party HTTP APIs from a Django app?
cards.back: A short answer that closes the question: I keep HTTP request logic in a separate client module rather than in views - that makes it easier to test and reuse. I make requests through requests with a mandatory timeout and raise_for_status(), so I don't hang on an unavailable service and catch errors explicitly. I pass keys and tokens through headers, and I take the secrets themselves from settings/environment rather than from the code. I cache frequent and slow responses through django.core.cache, setting a reasonable TTL. This set is enough to show that you think about reliability and structure, not just that you can call requests.get.

cards.front: Rate limiting: what it's for and which algorithms implement it?
cards.back: Rate limiting is needed so that the service doesn't choke under a flood of requests and doesn't suffer from overuse by individual clients. I'd name three reasons: protecting infrastructure from overload, deflecting parasitic and attacking traffic, and a fair distribution of quotas. The classic algorithms are Fixed Window (a counter within a fixed interval), Sliding Window (a moving window based on timestamps), and Token Bucket (a bucket of tokens refilled at a constant rate). The choice depends on how smoothly you need to flatten bursts: Token Bucket allows short spikes, while Sliding Window gives a more even flow.

cards.front: API versioning: what approaches exist and where is their boundary?
cards.back: I distinguish three approaches. The first is the version in the path (/v1/resource): it's visible right away, easy to cache, and trivial to route, but with large divergences it leads to duplicated handlers. The second is the version in a header, more often via Accept: application/vnd.example.v1+json: this is the "correct" approach from a REST standpoint through content negotiation, the URL stays clean, but the version isn't visible in the address and is harder to debug from a browser. The third is the version in a query parameter (?version=1): flexible, but the parameter is easy to lose and it clutters the routing logic. In practice, for public APIs people more often take the version in the path for predictability; the key idea is that any incompatible change must be moved to a new version, while compatible ones are changed without it.

cards.front: The access/refresh pair: how to renew a session without logging in again?
cards.back: The access token is made short-lived (for example, 15 minutes) so that if it leaks, the window for abuse is small. But forcing the user to log in every 15 minutes is inconvenient, so on sign-in the server issues a pair: access and refresh. The access token rides along in every request to the API; when it expires, the client sends the refresh token to a separate endpoint and gets a fresh access token. The refresh token lives longer and is therefore stored as securely as possible — usually in an httpOnly cookie inaccessible from JavaScript. A good answer adds: the refresh token should be made single-use with rotation (each renewal issues a new refresh token and invalidates the old one) and the server should keep a revocation list so a session can be killed.

cards.front: Where to place the token in an HTTP request, and which way to prefer?
cards.back: I'll name three places and prioritize them right away. The first and preferred one is the Authorization header with the Bearer scheme: the token doesn't end up in the URL, which means it doesn't settle into server logs, browser history, or the Referer header. The second is a query parameter (?access_token=...): workable but insecure, because the URL is logged and cached; I use it only when a header isn't available. The third is the request body: the token isn't in the URL, but this way works only for requests with a body (POST and the like) and breaks the idea of stateless authentication, where the token is expected in the header on every request. Bottom line: by default Authorization: Bearer, the rest are forced exceptions.

cards.front: When should you choose gRPC over REST for service-to-service communication?
cards.back: "I choose gRPC for internal traffic between microservices, where speed and low latency are critical. Messages are described in .proto and serialized into a compact binary format that is more economical than JSON, while HTTP/2 provides multiplexing and streaming in both directions. From a single contract, type-safe code is generated for different languages. REST I leave for public APIs that need to be human-readable and accessible directly from the browser."

cards.front: What HTTP methods exist and what is each responsible for?
cards.back: "There are a few core verbs. GET reads data and changes nothing — it is safe and idempotent. POST sends data and usually creates a resource; calling it again produces a new object, so it is not idempotent. PUT replaces a resource entirely and is idempotent: no matter how many times you repeat it, the state is the same. PATCH changes only the fields you send. DELETE removes a resource and is also idempotent. There is also HEAD — like GET but without a body — and OPTIONS, which tells you which methods the server supports."

cards.front: How do you catch webhooks on a local machine during development?
cards.back: "A webhook is an outbound HTTP request that a third-party service sends to my URL when an event occurs. For it to reach my machine, I bring up a tunnel: I run ngrok on the same port as the local server, get a public address like https://abcd.ngrok.io, and register it as the webhook URL in the external service's settings. All requests flow to my localhost, and in the ngrok web panel I see every request and can replay it — debugging takes minutes."

cards.front: What do the groups of HTTP status codes mean and which are the key ones?
cards.back: "Codes split into five groups by the first digit. 2xx means everything is fine: 200 OK, 201 Created when a resource is created, 204 No Content with no body. 3xx means another step is needed: 301 — moved permanently, 304 — not changed since last time. 4xx means the client is at fault: 400 — malformed request, 401 — authentication needed, 403 — no permission, 404 — resource not found. 5xx means something broke on the server: 500 — generic error, 502 — a bad response from upstream, 503 — service temporarily unavailable. The key distinction is that the client fixes 4xx, while 5xx is fixed on the backend."

cards.front: Which set of HTTP methods do clients use and what is each responsible for
cards.back: I'd put it this way: the request method states the client's intent. GET reads a resource and changes nothing, POST creates a new entity or triggers an action, PUT replaces a resource entirely, PATCH edits only individual fields, DELETE removes. HEAD (like GET, but the server returns only headers, no body) and OPTIONS (to find out which methods are allowed for a resource at all) stand apart. It is important to keep the methods' properties in mind: GET, HEAD, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent, GET and HEAD are also safe (they do not change state), while POST and PATCH are generally neither.

cards.front: What does the letter S add to HTTPS compared with plain HTTP
cards.back: I'd answer like this: HTTP on its own sends everything as plain text over port 80, and anyone along the route can read or tamper with the data. HTTPS wraps HTTP in a TLS layer (formerly called SSL) and works on port 443. TLS gives me three things: confidentiality — the traffic is encrypted; authenticity — the certificate proves I'm talking to exactly the intended server and not a man in the middle; integrity — any byte tampering along the way will be detected. The syntax of requests and methods itself does not change; only the transport underneath them does.

cards.front: How to lock cookies down against theft and forgery: flags and practices
cards.back: I'd describe the layers of protection. First — the HttpOnly flag: it blocks access to the cookie from document.cookie, so even under XSS a script cannot steal the session identifier. Second — Secure: the cookie is sent only over HTTPS, so it cannot be intercepted on an open channel. Third — SameSite (Lax or Strict): the browser does not attach the cookie to requests from other sites, which hits CSRF. Additionally, I limit the lifetime via Max-Age/Expires, do not put raw secrets in the cookie, and instead store an opaque session identifier tied to server-side state. These measures complement each other rather than replace one another.

cards.front: Who you are versus what you may do: separating authentication and authorization
cards.back: I'd put it briefly: authentication answers the question "who are you", authorization — "what may you do". First the user proves their identity, for example with a login and password, a token, or biometrics. That is authentication. Then the system looks at their role or set of permissions and decides which actions and resources are open to them — that is authorization. The order matters: authorization always comes after successful authentication; you cannot check the permissions of someone whose identity we have not yet established. At the HTTP level this shows up in the codes: 401 Unauthorized means "not authenticated", while 403 Forbidden means "identity is clear, but no permission".

cards.front: RPC and gRPC: how Google's remote call beats the classic scheme
cards.back: RPC (Remote Procedure Call) is a style of inter-service communication where invoking code on a remote server looks, to the developer, like a local call: arguments are serialized, fly over the network, the server runs the procedure and returns the result. gRPC is a modern RPC implementation: HTTP/2 transport gives multiplexing and bidirectional streams, messages are encoded into compact binary protobuf instead of text JSON, the contract is described in a .proto and generates client/server for a dozen languages at once. The result — lower latency, less traffic, and a strict contract out of the box.

cards.front: WebSocket: a persistent two-way channel instead of endlessly polling the server
cards.back: WebSocket is a protocol for bidirectional communication over a single TCP connection. The connection starts as an ordinary HTTP request with an Upgrade: websocket header; the server replies with 101 Switching Protocols, after which the channel switches into duplex mode and stays open. Unlike HTTP's request-response model, the server can push data to the client on its own, without waiting for a request. That is why WebSocket is chosen wherever instant two-way exchange is needed and classic polling is inefficient.

cards.front: Controlling the HTTP cache: which headers decide whether to store a response and how fresh it is
cards.back: The HTTP cache is controlled through response headers. The main one is Cache-Control, with the directives max-age, no-cache, no-store, public, private. The older Expires sets an absolute expiration date and exists for HTTP/1.0 compatibility. To validate a stale copy there are two validators: ETag (a fingerprint of the resource version) and Last-Modified (the change date). The client re-checks with conditional requests If-None-Match and If-Modified-Since, and if nothing has changed, the server replies 304 Not Modified with no body. This saves bandwidth, time, and server load.

cards.front: JWT from the inside: the token's three parts, the signature, and why the server can skip storing sessions
cards.back: A JWT is a self-contained token that parties use to exchange information as signed JSON. It consists of three dot-separated parts: header (type and algorithm), payload (claims — assertions about the user), and signature. The first two parts are just Base64URL; anyone can read them — it is the signature that protects them. The server issues the token after login, the client sends it in Authorization: Bearer , the server verifies the signature with a secret (HS256) or a public key (RS256) and trusts the contents. No state is kept on the server — this simplifies horizontal scaling and SSO.

cards.front: SOAP vs REST: where is the strict protocol, and where is the architectural style?
cards.back: I'd put it this way: SOAP is a protocol, REST is an architectural style, and that is the key difference. SOAP mandates a required XML envelope (Header plus Body), is transport-independent (HTTP, SMTP, TCP), and brings along standards like WS-Security and WS-ReliableMessaging — hence its place in banking and enterprise, where guarantees matter. REST operates on resources via URIs and standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), the body format is free, usually JSON. That is why REST is lighter, is cacheable with HTTP's own tools, and scales more easily — and it became the standard for public APIs.

cards.front: Why does the browser send a preliminary OPTIONS before the main request?
cards.back: An OPTIONS before the main request is a CORS preflight. The browser does it automatically when the request goes to a different origin and is not considered "simple" (for example, a POST with Content-Type: application/json or with custom headers). In the preflight the browser includes Access-Control-Request-Method and Access-Control-Request-Headers, and the server must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, and Access-Control-Allow-Headers. If the response permits it, the browser sends the real request; if not, it blocks it before it is even sent. This is a security mechanism of the browser itself, not the server.

cards.front: What are cookies and what role do they play in how sites work?
cards.back: Cookies solve the stateless nature of HTTP. The server sends a Set-Cookie header, the browser remembers it and then automatically sends it back in the Cookie header on every request to that domain. The main use is sessions and authentication: after login, a session identifier or token is placed in a cookie, and the server uses it to recognize the user. They are also used for personalization (language, theme) and analytics or advertising. Cookies have security attributes: HttpOnly hides the cookie from JavaScript, Secure sends it only over HTTPS, and SameSite limits cross-domain sending and protects against CSRF.

cards.front: What techniques speed up a service under a flood of HTTP requests?
cards.back: I'd go layer by layer. At the application level, for I/O-bound load I take async (asyncio plus aiohttp): a single thread serves thousands of connections without idling on network waits. At the transport level — HTTP/2: multiplexing sends many requests over one connection and removes the head-of-line blocking from HTTP/1.1. Next is caching: frequently requested data is served from a cache (client, server, CDN/proxy) without bothering the backend. Compression (Gzip/Brotli) reduces the response size and delivery time. And horizontally — a balancer (Nginx, HAProxy) spreads requests across several instances, adding both throughput and fault tolerance.

cards.front: How the TCP/IP stack is built and who is responsible for what in it
cards.back: TCP/IP is a stack of four layers: link, internet, transport, and application. The application layer is the protocols of programs (HTTP, SMTP, FTP). The transport layer offers two delivery options: TCP with guaranteed ordering and reliability, or UDP with no guarantees for the sake of speed. The internet layer is IP, which routes packets between nodes by IP address. The link layer handles physical transmission over Ethernet or Wi-Fi. On sending, data is encapsulated top-down; on receiving, it is unwrapped bottom-up.

cards.front: UDP: fast, connectionless delivery with no guarantees
cards.back: UDP is a connectionless transport protocol. Unlike TCP, it does not establish a connection before sending, does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or freedom from duplicates, and does not manage flow or congestion. In exchange it has almost no overhead, so it delivers low latency. It is chosen for streaming video and audio, online games, VoIP, and DNS queries - cases where delivering fresh data quickly matters more than reliably delivering stale data. If an application needs reliability on top of UDP, it implements that itself.

cards.front: URI vs URL: what the difference is and why it is not symmetric
cards.back: A URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) is the umbrella concept: anything that uniquely identifies a resource. Within URIs there are two kinds - URL and URN. A URL (Locator) specifies the location and access method: the scheme, host, and path. A URN (Name) specifies a stable name for the resource without tying it to a location, for example urn:isbn:0451450523. The key idea: the relationship is asymmetric - a URL is always a URI, but a URI can also be a URN, that is, not a URL. In practice on the web, almost everything we see in the address bar is a URL.

cards.front: Anatomy of an HTTP request: start line, headers, body
cards.back: An HTTP request is a text message made of three blocks. The first is the start line: the method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), the request target (the path to the resource), and the protocol version (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2). The second is the headers in "key: value" format, each on its own line: Host, User-Agent, Content-Type, and so on. The third is the body, which is not always present: a GET usually has none, while POST and PUT carry the data being sent in it. Between the headers and the body sits an empty separator line.

cards.front: GET, POST, and HEAD: where each method has its own area of responsibility
cards.back: GET fetches a resource from the server: parameters travel in the URL query string, the request does not change server state, so it is idempotent and cacheable. POST sends data in the body, suits large and private payloads, and is non-idempotent and not cacheable by default. HEAD is a GET without a response body: the server returns headers only, which is handy when you need only a resource's metadata (size, date, existence) and do not want to download the content itself.

cards.front: SSL and TLS: how an encrypted channel protects traffic and how they differ
cards.back: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) is the historical protocol; its versions are compromised and no longer in use. TLS (Transport Layer Security) is its successor, and it is exactly what stands behind every HTTPS connection, even though people still say "SSL certificate" out of habit. The protocol solves three problems: confidentiality (the traffic is encrypted), authentication (the certificate confirms the server is who it claims to be), and integrity (tampering with data in transit shows up via a MAC/hash). The connection starts with a handshake: the parties agree on ciphers, the server presents a certificate, and a session key is negotiated for fast symmetric encryption.

cards.front: REST as a style and RESTful as an implementation: what exactly the difference is
cards.back: REST (Representational State Transfer) is a set of architectural constraints: client-server, statelessness, cacheability, a uniform interface, and layering. The word "RESTful" means that a concrete API is designed according to these constraints. So REST is the theory and the style, while RESTful is an adjective describing an actual implementation. In practice a REST API addresses resources through URIs (/users, /users/42) and operates on them via HTTP methods: GET reads, POST creates, PUT/PATCH updates, DELETE removes. The key idea of statelessness: each request is self-contained, the server does not remember the client between requests, which simplifies scaling.

cards.front: HTTP caching: which headers let the browser decide when not to redownload a file
cards.back: There are two mechanisms. The first is freshness: Cache-Control: max-age=N (or the deprecated Expires) says how many seconds the copy may be served from the cache without contacting the server. The second is validation: when the freshness window has expired, the client does not download the file blindly but makes a conditional request with If-None-Match (based on ETag) or If-Modified-Since (based on Last-Modified). If the resource has not changed, the server replies 304 Not Modified with an empty body, and the old copy is used. Cache-Control is more flexible than Expires because it sets a relative time and the directives no-cache, no-store, public, and private.

cards.front: WebSocket vs a raw TCP/UDP socket: where the line is drawn
cards.back: These are different levels of abstraction, not competitors. A socket is an operating-system file descriptor through which you send bytes over TCP or UDP; everything else (where one message ends and another begins, the handshake, reconnection) is your responsibility. WebSocket sits above it: it starts as an ordinary HTTP request with Upgrade: websocket, and after a successful upgrade the same TCP connection switches into a mode of exchanging discrete message frames in both directions. So WebSocket almost always runs on top of a TCP socket, but it adds a handshake, message framing, and compatibility with web infrastructure (ports 80/443, proxies, the browser). A raw socket is chosen when you need full control or UDP; WebSocket - when you need a cheap two-way real-time channel from the browser.
